readingPassages.title: Section A - 选词填空
readingPassages.content: The concept of the circular economy has gained considerable traction in recent years as a potential solution to the environmental crises caused by traditional linear models of production and consumption. Unlike the conventional take-make-dispose approach, a circular economy aims to (1)______ waste and keep resources in use for as long as possible. This is achieved through strategies such as recycling, remanufacturing, and product life extension. Proponents argue that this model not only reduces environmental (2)______ but also creates economic opportunities. The transition to a circular economy requires fundamental changes across multiple (3)______ of the economy. Product designers must consider the entire lifecycle of a product, making it easier to repair, upgrade, and eventually recycle. Manufacturers need to adopt new business models that (4)______ access over ownership, such as leasing and product-as-a-service arrangements. Consumers, too, must shift their mindsets and embrace more (5)______ patterns of consumption. Several European countries have been at the forefront of this transition. The Netherlands, for instance, has (6)______ to become fully circular by 2050, while the European Union has adopted a Circular Economy Action Plan as part of its Green Deal. These initiatives include measures to (7)______ recyclability, reduce single-use plastics, and combat planned obsolescence. However, significant (8)______ remain. The upfront costs of redesigning products and retooling factories can be substantial. Current economic incentives often favor linear models, and recycling (9)______ in many countries are inadequate. Furthermore, the global nature of supply chains makes it difficult to implement circular principles on a wide scale. Despite these obstacles, the momentum behind the circular economy continues to build, driven by growing (10)______ of the environmental and economic costs of our current wasteful systems.

questions.questionText: aims to (1)______ waste and keep resources in use
questions.explanation: minimize意为"最小化"，循环经济的目标是最小化浪费，与后文"尽可能长时间保持资源使用"呼应。六级词汇考查更注重学术语境。
questions.translation: 循环经济旨在最大限度地减少浪费并尽可能长时间地保持资源使用。

questions.questionText: reduces environmental (2)______
questions.explanation: impact意为"影响"，environmental impact是学术写作中的高频搭配，表示"环境影响"。
questions.translation: 这种模式不仅减少环境影响，还创造经济机会。

questions.questionText: across multiple (3)______ of the economy
questions.explanation: sectors意为"部门、领域"，multiple sectors of the economy指"经济的多个部门"。
questions.translation: 向循环经济转型需要在经济的多个领域进行根本性变革。

questions.questionText: business models that (4)______ access over ownership
questions.explanation: prioritize意为"优先考虑"，商业模式优先考虑使用权而非所有权，如租赁模式。
questions.translation: 制造商需要采用优先考虑使用权而非所有权的商业模式。

questions.questionText: embrace more (5)______ patterns of consumption
questions.explanation: sustainable意为"可持续的"，sustainable patterns of consumption是六级常考搭配。
questions.translation: 消费者也必须转变思维，接受更可持续的消费模式。

questions.questionText: has (6)______ to become fully circular by 2050
questions.explanation: pledged意为"承诺"，荷兰承诺到2050年实现完全循环经济，与四级词汇相比，pledged在六级中更强调正式承诺。
questions.translation: 荷兰承诺到2050年实现完全循环经济。

questions.questionText: measures to (7)______ recyclability
questions.explanation: enhance意为"提高、增强"，enhance recyclability指"提高可回收性"。
questions.translation: 这些措施包括提高可回收性、减少一次性塑料和打击计划性报废。

questions.questionText: significant (8)______ remain
questions.explanation: barriers意为"障碍"，significant barriers remain表示"仍然存在重大障碍"。
questions.translation: 然而，仍然存在重大障碍。

questions.questionText: recycling (9)______ in many countries are inadequate
questions.explanation: infrastructure意为"基础设施"，recycling infrastructure指"回收基础设施"。
questions.translation: 许多国家的回收基础设施不足。

questions.questionText: driven by growing (10)______ of the environmental and economic costs
questions.explanation: awareness意为"意识"，growing awareness表示"日益增长的意识"，驱动循环经济势头的动力。
questions.translation: 受人们对当前浪费系统的环境和经济成本日益增长的认识所驱动。

readingPassages.title: Section B - 长篇阅读匹配
readingPassages.content:
A) The phenomenon of digital nomadism has emerged as one of the most distinctive lifestyle trends of the twenty-first century. Digital nomads are individuals who leverage technology to work remotely while traveling and living in different locations around the world. This lifestyle, once confined to a small group of freelancers and entrepreneurs, has become increasingly mainstream as advances in communication technology and changes in work culture have made location-independent work more feasible than ever before.

B) The digital nomad lifestyle offers several compelling advantages. Freedom and flexibility are the most frequently cited benefits. Digital nomads have the autonomy to choose where and when they work, allowing them to design their ideal lifestyle. Many report higher levels of job satisfaction and creativity, attributing these benefits to the diverse experiences and perspectives gained through constant exposure to new environments and cultures.

C) However, the digital nomad lifestyle also presents significant challenges that are often overlooked. Loneliness and social isolation are common complaints, as maintaining meaningful relationships becomes difficult when constantly on the move. The lack of a stable community can take a toll on mental health. Furthermore, the blurring of boundaries between work and leisure can lead to burnout, as the temptation to always be working is ever-present when ones office is also ones home.

D) Financial considerations are another important aspect of digital nomadism. While the lifestyle can be more affordable than living in expensive urban centers, it comes with its own set of financial complexities. Navigating different tax systems, dealing with currency fluctuations, and managing income without a steady employer can be challenging. Health insurance is a particular concern, as standard policies often do not cover individuals living abroad for extended periods.

E) The rise of digital nomadism has not gone unnoticed by governments and policymakers. Several countries have introduced specific visa programs designed to attract digital nomads. Portugal, Estonia, and Costa Rica are among the nations that have launched digital nomad visas, allowing remote workers to live and work in the country for one to two years. These programs are typically designed to boost local economies, as digital nomads tend to spend money on accommodation, dining, and local services.

F) Critics argue that digital nomadism can have negative impacts on local communities. In some popular destinations, the influx of relatively wealthy digital nomads has contributed to rising housing costs and gentrification, pricing out local residents. There are also concerns about the environmental impact of frequent air travel, which is often a central feature of the digital nomad lifestyle. The carbon footprint of a digital nomad who flies to a new destination every few months can be substantial.

G) The demographics of digital nomads are shifting. While early digital nomads were predominantly young, single, and male, the community has become increasingly diverse. More families, couples, and older individuals are embracing the lifestyle. Companies are also taking notice, with some offering work-from-anywhere policies as a benefit to attract and retain talent. A survey by MBO Partners found that the number of digital nomads in the United States grew by over 50 percent between 2020 and 2022.

H) Technology infrastructure is a critical enabler of digital nomadism. Reliable high-speed internet is the most essential requirement, and destinations that offer good connectivity have a competitive advantage. Coworking spaces have proliferated in cities around the world, providing digital nomads with dedicated workspaces and opportunities for social interaction. Tools like Slack, Zoom, and project management software have made remote collaboration seamless across time zones.

I) The future of digital nomadism will likely involve greater structure and professionalization. As more people adopt this lifestyle, a supporting ecosystem of services is emerging, including specialized insurance products, tax advisory services, and community platforms. Some experts predict that digital nomadism will evolve from a niche lifestyle into a standard mode of work for a significant portion of the workforce, particularly in knowledge-intensive industries.

J) Ultimately, digital nomadism represents a fundamental rethinking of the relationship between work, place, and identity. It challenges the traditional assumption that one must live where one works and offers a vision of a more flexible and personalized approach to professional life. While it is not suitable for everyone and comes with genuine challenges, the growing interest in this lifestyle reflects broader shifts in how we think about work and quality of life in the digital age.

questions.questionText: Digital nomads often experience loneliness and social isolation due to constant travel.
questions.explanation: 段落C指出数字游民生活方式带来显著挑战，孤独感和社交孤立是常见的抱怨。
questions.translation: 数字游民因不断旅行常经历孤独和社交孤立。

questions.questionText: Several countries have introduced special visas to attract digital nomads and boost local economies.
questions.explanation: 段落E提到葡萄牙、爱沙尼亚和哥斯达黎加等国推出了数字游民签证，旨在吸引远程工作者并促进本地经济。
questions.translation: 多个国家推出了特殊签证以吸引数字游民并促进本地经济。

questions.questionText: The digital nomad community has become more diverse, including more families and older individuals.
questions.explanation: 段落G指出数字游民的人口结构正在变化，越来越多的家庭、伴侣和年长者加入这一生活方式。
questions.translation: 数字游民社区变得更加多样化，包括更多的家庭和年长者。

questions.questionText: Critics are concerned about digital nomads contributing to rising housing costs in popular destinations.
questions.explanation: 段落F指出批评者认为数字游民的涌入推高了住房成本，导致中产阶级化，使当地居民负担不起。
questions.translation: 批评者担心数字游民在热门目的地推高了住房成本。

questions.questionText: High-speed internet is the most essential requirement for digital nomads.
questions.explanation: 段落H指出可靠的高速互联网是最基本的要求，提供良好网络连接的目的地具有竞争优势。
questions.translation: 高速互联网是数字游民最基本的要求。

questions.questionText: Digital nomadism challenges the traditional assumption that one must live where one works.
questions.explanation: 段落J总结指出数字游民生活方式挑战了必须在工作地点居住的传统假设。
questions.translation: 数字游民生活方式挑战了必须在工作地居住的传统假设。

questions.questionText: Digital nomads have the freedom to choose when and where they work.
questions.explanation: 段落B指出自由和灵活性是数字游民最常提到的好处，他们可以自主选择工作时间和地点。
questions.translation: 数字游民可以自由选择工作时间和地点。

questions.questionText: The number of digital nomads in the United States grew significantly between 2020 and 2022.
questions.explanation: 段落G引用MBO Partners调查数据，指出2020年至2022年间美国数字游民数量增长了超过50%。
questions.translation: 2020年至2022年间，美国数字游民数量显著增长。

questions.questionText: Digital nomadism is made possible by advances in communication technology and changing work culture.
questions.explanation: 段落A指出通信技术的进步和工作文化的变化使不受地点限制的工作比以往任何时候都更加可行。
questions.translation: 通信技术的进步和工作文化的变化使数字游民生活方式成为可能。

questions.questionText: Digital nomads face financial challenges such as navigating different tax systems and obtaining health insurance.
questions.explanation: 段落D提到数字游民需要应对不同税收体系、货币波动以及没有固定雇主时的收入管理，医疗保险尤其令人担忧。
questions.translation: 数字游民面临应对不同税收体系和获取医疗保险等财务挑战。

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage One
readingPassages.content:
The relationship between economic growth and environmental sustainability has long been a subject of intense debate. For decades, the dominant paradigm held that economic development inevitably comes at the expense of the environment. However, the concept of green growth has emerged as an alternative framework, arguing that economic expansion and environmental protection can be mutually reinforcing rather than mutually exclusive.

Green growth, as defined by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, involves fostering economic growth and development while ensuring that natural assets continue to provide the resources and environmental services on which our well-being relies. This approach emphasizes the potential of technological innovation to decouple economic growth from resource use and environmental degradation. Investments in renewable energy, energy efficiency, and sustainable agriculture are seen as drivers of both economic prosperity and environmental protection.

Proponents of green growth point to examples from countries that have successfully reduced emissions while maintaining economic growth. Denmark, for instance, has grown its economy by over 70 percent since 1990 while keeping carbon emissions relatively stable through massive investments in wind energy and energy efficiency. Similarly, Costa Rica has managed to reverse deforestation while expanding its economy, demonstrating that conservation and development need not be in conflict.

Critics, however, argue that green growth is an oxymoron. They contend that the scale of economic activity on the planet has already exceeded planetary boundaries, and that true sustainability requires a fundamental restructuring of the economy away from growth as the primary objective. The concept of degrowth advocates for a planned reduction of production and consumption in wealthy nations to bring human activity back within ecological limits. These critics argue that technological efficiency gains have historically been offset by increased consumption, a phenomenon known as the rebound effect.

The empirical evidence on green growth remains mixed. While some countries have achieved relative decoupling of emissions from growth, absolute decoupling on a global scale has proven elusive. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change has emphasized that without significant changes in consumption patterns, technological improvements alone will not be sufficient to meet climate targets. This suggests that while green growth may be part of the solution, it cannot be the only strategy. A combination of technological innovation, behavioral change, and policy reform will likely be necessary to achieve genuine sustainability.

questions.questionText: What is the traditional view of the relationship between economic growth and the environment?
options: A. Economic growth and environmental protection are mutually reinforcing.
B. Economic development inevitably harms the environment.
C. The environment has no relationship with economic growth.
D. Environmental protection always slows economic growth.
questions.explanation: 第一段指出传统的观点认为经济发展不可避免地以环境为代价，这与绿色增长理念形成对比。
questions.translation: 经济增长与环境关系的传统观点是什么？

questions.questionText: How does the OECD define green growth?
options: A. Slowing economic growth to protect the environment.
B. Fostering growth while ensuring natural assets continue to provide resources.
C. Prioritizing environmental protection over all economic activities.
D. Using only renewable energy sources for all production.
questions.explanation: 第二段引用OECD定义：绿色增长是在促进经济增长和发展的同时，确保自然资源持续提供我们福祉所需的资源和服务。
questions.translation: OECD如何定义绿色增长？

questions.questionText: What example does the author give to support the green growth argument?
options: A. Denmark grew its economy while keeping emissions stable.
B. The United States reduced emissions without any economic cost.
C. China achieved rapid growth by ignoring environmental concerns.
D. Germany eliminated all carbon emissions from its economy.
questions.explanation: 第三段以丹麦为例，自1990年以来经济增长超过70%，同时通过对风能和能效的大规模投资保持了碳排放的相对稳定。
questions.translation: 作者举了哪个例子来支持绿色增长的论点？

questions.questionText: What is the degrowth argument according to critics?
options: A. Economic growth should continue at any cost.
B. Wealthy nations need to reduce production and consumption.
C. Developing countries should not pursue economic development.
D. Technology alone can solve all environmental problems.
questions.explanation: 第四段指出"去增长"概念主张富裕国家有计划地减少生产和消费，使人类活动回到生态极限之内。
questions.translation: 根据批评者的观点，去增长论点是什么？

questions.questionText: What does the author conclude about achieving genuine sustainability?
options: A. Green growth alone is sufficient.
B. Technological innovation is the only solution.
C. A combination of innovation, behavior change, and policy reform is needed.
D. Economic growth must be completely stopped.
questions.explanation: 最后一段作者总结认为，技术创新、行为改变和政策改革的结合才可能实现真正的可持续发展。
questions.translation: 作者对实现真正的可持续发展得出了什么结论？

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage Two
readingPassages.content:
The neuroscience of decision-making has revealed fascinating insights into how the human brain evaluates choices and arrives at conclusions. Far from being the purely rational process that classical economics assumed, decision-making is profoundly influenced by emotions, cognitive biases, and social context. Understanding these influences has important implications for everything from public policy to personal finance.

One of the most influential frameworks for understanding decision-making is Daniel Kahnemans dual-process theory, which distinguishes between System 1 and System 2 thinking. System 1 is fast, intuitive, and emotional, operating automatically with little effort. System 2 is slow, deliberate, and analytical, requiring conscious effort and attention. Kahneman demonstrated that while we like to think of ourselves as rational decision-makers, System 1 actually drives most of our daily choices, with System 2 serving more as a rationalizer of decisions already made than as the primary decision-maker.

Cognitive biases systematically affect the decisions we make. Confirmation bias leads us to seek out information that confirms our existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence. The availability heuristic causes us to overestimate the likelihood of events that are easily recalled, such as plane crashes, while underestimating more common but less dramatic risks. Loss aversion, a key concept in prospect theory, suggests that the pain of losing is psychologically about twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining. This asymmetry can lead to irrational decisions, such as holding onto losing investments in the hope of breaking even.

Social context exerts a powerful influence on decision-making. The phenomenon of groupthink occurs when the desire for harmony in a group leads to poor decisions, as dissenting opinions are suppressed. Social proof, or the tendency to follow the behavior of others, can lead to both beneficial behaviors, such as adopting sustainable practices, and harmful ones, such as participating in speculative bubbles. The rise of social media has amplified these social influences, with algorithms designed to maximize engagement often reinforcing existing biases and creating echo chambers.

Understanding these cognitive mechanisms offers pathways to better decision-making. Techniques such as considering the opposite, seeking out disconfirming evidence, and creating decision-making checklists can help mitigate the influence of biases. In public policy, concepts from behavioral economics have been used to design choice architectures that nudge people toward better decisions without restricting their freedom. However, there are ethical concerns about the extent to which governments and corporations should be allowed to influence our choices, even for our own benefit.

questions.questionText: What does classical economics assume about decision-making?
options: A. It is driven primarily by emotions.
B. It is a purely rational process.
C. It is influenced by social context.
D. It is mostly unconscious.
questions.explanation: 第一段指出传统经济学假设决策是纯粹理性的过程，而神经科学发现事实并非如此。
questions.translation: 传统经济学对决策的假设是什么？

questions.questionText: According to Kahneman, which system drives most of our daily choices?
options: A. System 1, which is fast and intuitive.
B. System 2, which is slow and analytical.
C. Both systems equally.
D. Neither system.
questions.explanation: 第二段指出Kahneman的双重过程理论中，系统1（快速、直觉、情绪化）驱动了我们大部分的日常选择。
questions.translation: 根据Kahneman的理论，哪个系统驱动了我们大部分的日常选择？

questions.questionText: What is loss aversion according to the passage?
options: A. The tendency to avoid making any decisions.
B. The idea that losses feel more painful than gains feel pleasurable.
C. The preference for certain outcomes over uncertain ones.
D. The tendency to sell winning investments too early.
questions.explanation: 第三段指出损失厌恶（loss aversion）意味着失去的痛苦在心理上大约是获得的快乐的两倍。
questions.translation: 根据文章，什么是损失厌恶？

questions.questionText: How has social media affected decision-making according to the passage?
options: A. It has reduced the influence of cognitive biases.
B. It has amplified social influences and reinforced biases.
C. It has made people more rational decision-makers.
D. It has eliminated groupthink entirely.
questions.explanation: 第三段指出社交媒体的兴起放大了社会影响，旨在最大化用户参与度的算法往往强化现有偏见并制造信息茧房。
questions.translation: 根据文章，社交媒体如何影响决策？

questions.questionText: What ethical concern is raised about using behavioral economics in public policy?
options: A. It is not effective in changing behavior.
B. It is too expensive to implement.
C. The extent to which choices should be influenced by governments.
D. It only benefits wealthy individuals.
questions.explanation: 最后一段指出关于政府和企业在多大程度上应该被允许影响我们的选择存在伦理方面的担忧。
questions.translation: 在公共政策中使用行为经济学引发了什么伦理担忧？

translation.chineseText: 中国新能源汽车产业近年来发展迅速，已成为全球最大的新能源汽车市场。中国政府出台了一系列政策措施，包括购车补贴、免征购置税和建设充电基础设施等，大力推动新能源汽车的普及。新能源汽车不仅减少了尾气排放，改善了空气质量，还推动了中国汽车产业的技术升级。多家中国汽车品牌在电池技术、智能驾驶等领域取得了世界领先的成就。随着环保意识的增强和技术的不断进步，新能源汽车正在改变人们的出行方式。
translation.englishAnswer: Chinas new energy vehicle industry has developed rapidly in recent years and has become the worlds largest market for new energy vehicles. The Chinese government has introduced a series of policies and measures, including purchase subsidies, exemption from purchase tax, and the construction of charging infrastructure, to vigorously promote the popularization of new energy vehicles. New energy vehicles not only reduce exhaust emissions and improve air quality but also promote the technological upgrading of Chinas automobile industry. Several Chinese automobile brands have achieved world-leading accomplishments in areas such as battery technology and intelligent driving. With the growing environmental awareness and continuous technological progress, new energy vehicles are changing the way people travel.
keyPoints: 词汇：new energy vehicles, subsidies, infrastructure, exhaust emissions
六级句式：not only...but also... 连接两个并列谓语
专业术语：battery technology（电池技术）, intelligent driving（智能驾驶）
复合结构：with the growing...（随着……的增长）
translation.explanation: 本题为六级翻译题，主题为中国新能源汽车产业发展。六级翻译比四级更注重句式复杂度和词汇深度。注意：1) "新能源汽车"译为new energy vehicles；2) "免征购置税"译为exemption from purchase tax；3) "充电基础设施"译为charging infrastructure；4) 注意长句的拆分与合并。

listeningSections.title: Conversation One
listeningSections.script:
W: Good morning, Professor Johnson. Thank you for agreeing to talk about your research on urban green spaces.
M: Good morning, Sarah. It's my pleasure. Urban green spaces have become increasingly important as cities grow denser.
W: Could you explain what exactly constitutes urban green spaces?
M: Well, they include parks, community gardens, green roofs, street trees, and even small pocket parks. Any vegetated area within an urban environment qualifies. Our research shows that access to these spaces significantly improves mental health outcomes for city residents.
W: That's fascinating. What specific benefits have you observed?
M: We've found that people living within a five-minute walk of a green space report 40% lower stress levels. Additionally, these areas promote physical activity and social interaction among neighbors. In one study, we observed that children who regularly play in green spaces show better cognitive development.
W: Are there any economic benefits as well?
M: Absolutely. Properties near well-maintained green spaces typically see a 5-15% increase in value. Cities also save money on stormwater management because green spaces absorb rainfall naturally. The return on investment for urban green space development is quite substantial.
W: What advice would you give to city planners?
M: I would emphasize that green spaces should be distributed equitably across all neighborhoods, not just affluent areas. Environmental justice is a crucial consideration in urban planning.

questions.translation: 这段对话的主要话题是什么？
questions.explanation: 对话中教授主要讨论城市绿地的定义和益处，包括心理健康、经济发展等方面。
options: A. Climate change impacts on cities
B. Benefits of urban green spaces
C. Urban transportation systems
D. Housing prices in cities
questions.questionText: What is the main topic of the conversation?

questions.translation: 研究显示了什么具体的心理健康益处？
questions.explanation: 教授提到住在绿地步行5分钟范围内的人报告压力水平降低40%。
options: A. 20% lower stress levels
B. 30% lower anxiety levels
C. 40% lower stress levels
D. 50% lower depression rates
questions.questionText: What specific mental health benefit does the research show?

questions.translation: 文中提到了绿地的什么经济益处？
questions.explanation: 教授提到维护良好的绿地附近的房产价值通常增长5-15%。
options: A. Reduced property taxes
B. Increased property values
C. Lower construction costs
D. Higher rental income for landlords
questions.questionText: What economic benefit of green spaces is mentioned?

questions.translation: 教授给城市规划者什么建议？
questions.explanation: 教授强调绿地应该公平分布到所有社区，而不仅仅是富裕地区，这涉及环境正义问题。
options: A. Focus on building more skyscrapers
B. Prioritize commercial development
C. Distribute green spaces equitably
D. Reduce public transportation funding
questions.questionText: What advice does the professor give to city planners?

listeningSections.title: Conversation Two
listeningSections.script:
M: Hi Lisa, I heard you just came back from the International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Ethics. How was it?
W: It was incredibly insightful, Tom. The conference brought together researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders from over 40 countries.
M: What were the main topics discussed?
W: The central theme was how to establish ethical guidelines for AI development. One of the key concerns is algorithmic bias. Several speakers presented cases where AI systems discriminated against certain groups in hiring, lending, and criminal justice.
M: That sounds serious. Are there any proposed solutions?
W: Yes, many. One approach is to ensure diverse representation in AI development teams. Another is to implement regular audits of AI systems for fairness. The EU's AI Act was frequently mentioned as a pioneering regulatory framework.
M: What about the impact on jobs? That's something many people worry about.
W: That was discussed extensively. Most experts agree that AI will transform jobs rather than eliminate them entirely. However, there's a consensus that significant investment in retraining programs is necessary. The conference emphasized the need for lifelong learning systems.
M: Did they talk about AI in healthcare?
W: Yes, that was a major focus. AI shows tremendous promise in medical diagnosis and drug discovery. However, concerns about patient privacy and data security were raised. The conference concluded that international cooperation is essential for developing responsible AI.

listeningSections.title: Passage One
listeningSections.script:
The concept of digital minimalism has gained popularity as a response to the overwhelming presence of technology in our daily lives. Coined by computer science professor Cal Newport, digital minimalism is a philosophy of technology use in which you focus your online time on a small number of carefully selected and optimized activities that strongly support things you value, and then happily miss out on everything else.

The principles of digital minimalism are rooted in the belief that intentionality is key to a healthy relationship with technology. Rather than allowing apps and platforms to dictate how we spend our attention, digital minimalists make conscious choices about which tools they use and how they use them. This approach stands in stark contrast to the prevailing culture of constant connectivity and notification-driven engagement.

Research supports the benefits of this approach. Studies have shown that reducing screen time, particularly social media use, leads to improved mental health outcomes, better sleep quality, and increased productivity. One study found that participants who took a one-week break from social media reported significant reductions in anxiety and depression symptoms.

Implementing digital minimalism doesn't require completely abandoning technology. Instead, it involves a process of evaluating which digital tools genuinely add value to your life and eliminating those that don't. This might mean turning off notifications, scheduling specific times for checking email, or designating technology-free periods during the day. The goal is not to reject technology but to use it as a tool that serves your values rather than as a master that controls your attention.

listeningSections.title: Passage Two
listeningSections.script:
The phenomenon of cultural globalization has transformed the way people around the world experience culture. While some celebrate the increased access to diverse cultural products, others worry about the homogenization of global culture. This tension between cultural exchange and cultural preservation is at the heart of contemporary debates about globalization.

One of the most visible aspects of cultural globalization is the spread of popular culture, particularly through films, music, and social media. Hollywood movies are watched in nearly every country, and American music dominates global streaming charts. However, this is not a one-way process. Korean pop music, or K-pop, has gained massive international followings, and Japanese anime has become a global phenomenon. Indian Bollywood films have audiences stretching from Nigeria to Canada.

The role of language in cultural globalization is particularly significant. English has become the global lingua franca in business, science, and technology. Yet this dominance raises concerns about linguistic diversity. Linguists estimate that one language dies every two weeks, taking with it an entire system of knowledge and cultural expression. Efforts to preserve endangered languages have gained momentum, with some countries implementing bilingual education programs.

Cultural globalization also affects food, fashion, and daily practices. Sushi restaurants can be found in small towns across Europe, and Mexican tacos are popular in Japan. This culinary exchange enriches local food cultures but can also lead to the commercialization and simplification of traditional cuisines. The challenge for societies today is to embrace the benefits of cultural exchange while maintaining the unique traditions that give communities their distinct identity.

questions.translation: 文化全球化争论中的核心张力是什么？
questions.explanation: 文化全球化争论的核心是文化交流与文化保护之间的张力。
options: A. Economic vs. cultural benefits

readingPassages.title: Section A - 选词填空
readingPassages.content: The phenomenon of misinformation spreading through social media has become a subject of intense academic scrutiny. Researchers have identified several mechanisms that (1)______ to the viral spread of false information. One key factor is that emotionally charged content tends to be shared more frequently than neutral information, regardless of its accuracy. The platforms algorithms, designed to (2)______ user engagement, often prioritize sensational content over factual reporting. This creates an environment where misinformation can spread rapidly, (3)______ efforts by fact-checkers to contain it. The consequences of this misinformation ecosystem are far-reaching. During the COVID-19 pandemic, false claims about treatments and vaccines (4)______, leading to real-world harm. Political misinformation has been shown to (5)______ democratic processes by undermining trust in institutions and amplifying social divisions. The economic costs are also (6)______, with fake news affecting stock markets and consumer behavior. Various solutions have been proposed to address this problem. Media literacy education aims to equip individuals with the skills to (7)______ credible sources from unreliable ones. Fact-checking organizations have grown in scale and sophistication, using AI tools to detect false claims more quickly. Platform companies have (8)______ content moderation policies, though these efforts have been criticized from all sides, with some arguing they do too little and others claiming they infringe on free speech. Legislative approaches have also been attempted. The European Unions Digital Services Act requires platforms to be more (9)______ about their algorithms and to take greater responsibility for harmful content on their services. However, regulating online speech raises complex legal and ethical questions. As research continues to (10)______ the complex dynamics of misinformation, it is clear that no single solution will be sufficient. A multi-faceted approach combining education, technology, regulation, and individual responsibility will be necessary.

readingPassages.title: Section B - 长篇阅读匹配
readingPassages.content:
A) Mental health has emerged as one of the most pressing public health challenges of our time. The World Health Organization estimates that one in four people will be affected by mental or neurological disorders at some point in their lives. Despite the prevalence of these conditions, stigma and inadequate funding have historically left mental health marginalized within healthcare systems. However, a growing awareness of the scale of the problem is driving change in how societies approach mental wellbeing.

B) The economic burden of mental illness is staggering. According to a study by the World Economic Forum, mental health conditions are projected to cost the global economy $16 trillion by 2030, surpassing the costs of cancer, diabetes, and respiratory diseases combined. These costs stem from reduced productivity, increased healthcare expenditure, and the social welfare costs associated with disability and unemployment. This economic argument has become a powerful catalyst for increased investment in mental health services.

C) Workplace mental health has become a particular focus of attention. The modern work environment, characterized by long hours, high pressure, and the blurring of boundaries between work and personal life due to digital technology, has contributed to rising rates of stress, anxiety, and burnout among employees. Many companies have responded by implementing employee assistance programs, offering mental health days, and training managers to recognize signs of psychological distress in their team members.

D) Digital technology is playing an increasingly important role in mental health care. Teletherapy platforms have made psychological counseling more accessible, particularly for those in rural or underserved areas. Smartphone apps offering cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness meditation, and mood tracking have put mental health tools directly into peoples pockets. However, concerns remain about the quality and efficacy of many digital mental health products, which are not always subject to the same regulatory standards as traditional treatments.

E) Young people are particularly vulnerable to mental health challenges. Rates of anxiety, depression, and self-harm among adolescents have increased significantly over the past decade. Social media is frequently cited as a contributing factor, with studies linking heavy social media use to poorer mental health outcomes. However, the relationship is complex, as social media can also provide support and connection for marginalized youth. The COVID-19 pandemic exacerbated these trends, with school closures and social isolation having lasting effects on the mental health of an entire generation.

F) There is growing recognition of the social determinants of mental health. Factors such as poverty, housing instability, discrimination, and exposure to violence significantly increase the risk of mental illness. This understanding has led to calls for a public health approach that addresses these root causes rather than focusing solely on individual treatment. Countries that have invested in social safety nets and reduced inequality tend to have better population mental health outcomes.

G) The treatment gap for mental health conditions remains enormous, particularly in low and middle-income countries. In these settings, there are fewer than one mental health professional per 100,000 people, compared to 40 per 100,000 in high-income countries. Task-sharing approaches, where non-specialist health workers are trained to deliver basic mental health care, have shown promise in bridging this gap. The integration of mental health services into primary healthcare is another strategy being pursued.

H) Prevention is increasingly recognized as a crucial component of mental health strategy. Evidence-based programs that build resilience, teach emotional regulation skills, and promote healthy lifestyles have been shown to reduce the incidence of mental health conditions. School-based programs that address bullying, promote social and emotional learning, and provide early intervention are particularly effective. Investment in prevention is not only ethically sound but also economically sensible, as every dollar spent on mental health prevention yields multiple dollars in returns.

I) The stigma surrounding mental health is slowly diminishing, thanks in large part to advocacy and awareness campaigns. High-profile individuals speaking openly about their own mental health struggles have helped normalize these conversations. Social media campaigns have encouraged people to share their experiences and seek help. However, stigma remains a significant barrier, particularly in some cultural contexts where mental illness is seen as a sign of personal weakness or brings shame to the family.

J) Looking to the future, there is reason for cautious optimism. The increased attention to mental health, combined with advances in treatment and technology, has the potential to transform mental health care. However, realizing this potential will require sustained political commitment, increased funding, and a willingness to address the social and economic factors that shape mental health. Mental health is not merely the absence of mental illness but a fundamental component of overall wellbeing that deserves the same priority as physical health.

questions.questionText: Mental health conditions are projected to cost the global economy $16 trillion by 2030.
questions.explanation: 段落B引用世界经济论坛研究，指出到2030年精神健康状况预计将给全球经济造成16万亿美元的损失。

questions.explanation: 段落A引用世界卫生组织估计，每四人中有一人会在生命某个阶段受到精神或神经疾病影响。

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage One
readingPassages.content:
The concept of universal basic income has moved from the fringes of political discourse to the mainstream in recent years, driven by concerns about job displacement due to automation and the inadequacy of existing social safety nets. UBI, as it is commonly known, involves providing all citizens with a regular, unconditional sum of money from the government, regardless of their employment status or income level.

Proponents of UBI advance several arguments in its favor. First, they contend that it would provide a more efficient and dignified form of social support than existing welfare systems, which are often bureaucratic, conditional, and stigmatizing. By eliminating means-testing and conditionality, UBI would reduce administrative costs and ensure that no one falls through the cracks. Second, advocates argue that UBI would promote economic security and enable people to take risks, such as starting a business, pursuing education, or leaving unsatisfactory jobs. Third, in an era of increasing automation, UBI is seen as a potential solution to the displacement of workers by machines, providing a basic level of economic security for all.

Several pilot programs have been conducted to test the effects of UBI. Perhaps the most famous is Finlands two-year experiment, which provided 2,000 unemployed individuals with a monthly payment of 560 euros. The results showed that recipients reported higher levels of wellbeing and slightly higher employment rates than the control group. Similar experiments in Kenya, Canada, and the United States have yielded generally positive results, though the规模和 duration of these programs have been limited.

Critics raise several objections to UBI. The most common concern is cost. Providing a meaningful basic income to all citizens would require substantial government expenditure, likely necessitating significant tax increases or cuts to other programs. Some economists argue that UBI could be inflationary, driving up prices without improving real living standards. Others worry that unconditional payments could reduce the incentive to work, though the evidence from pilot programs does not strongly support this concern.

There is also debate about whether UBI should replace or complement existing welfare programs. Some advocates envision UBI as a replacement for the current patchwork of social programs, arguing that this would simplify the system and reduce bureaucracy. Others see UBI as a floor on which other targeted programs should be built, particularly for groups with specific needs such as people with disabilities. The design details, including the amount of the payment, its frequency, and its relationship to existing programs, are crucial to determining the actual impact of any UBI scheme.

questions.questionText: What has driven the concept of UBI into the mainstream according to the passage?
options: A. Political campaigns and election promises.
B. Concerns about automation and inadequate social safety nets.
C. Successful implementation in several countries.
D. Pressure from international organizations.
questions.explanation: 第一段指出对自动化导致的失业的担忧以及现有社会安全网的不足，推动UBI从边缘进入主流。
questions.translation: 根据文章，什么因素将UBI概念推向了主流？

questions.questionText: What advantage of UBI do proponents highlight?
options: A. It would eliminate all forms of taxation.
B. It would reduce administrative costs and provide dignified support.
C. It would replace all other forms of government spending.
D. It would encourage people to work longer hours.
questions.explanation: 第二段指出支持者认为UBI比现有福利系统更高效、更有尊严，通过取消经济状况调查和条件限制来减少行政成本。
questions.translation: 支持者强调了UBI的什么优势？

questions.questionText: What did Finlands UBI experiment find?
options: A. Recipients had lower employment rates.
B. Recipients reported higher wellbeing and slightly higher employment rates.
C. The program was too expensive to continue.
D. Recipients stopped looking for work entirely.
questions.explanation: 第三段指出芬兰的试验结果显示，受助者报告了更高的幸福感和略高于对照组的就业率。
questions.translation: 芬兰的UBI实验发现了什么？

questions.questionText: What is the most common concern raised by critics of UBI?
options: A. It would reduce the quality of public services.
B. It would be too costly to implement.
C. It would benefit only the wealthy.
D. It would be difficult to administer.
questions.explanation: 第四段指出最常见的担忧是成本问题，提供有意义的全民基本收入需要大量政府支出。
questions.translation: 批评者对UBI提出的最常见担忧是什么？

questions.questionText: What debate exists regarding the implementation of UBI?
options: A. Whether it should be funded by taxes or borrowing.
B. Whether it should replace or complement existing welfare programs.
C. Whether it should be paid monthly or annually.
D. Whether it should apply to citizens only or all residents.
questions.explanation: 最后一段指出关于UBI是否应该取代现有福利计划还是作为补充存在争议。
questions.translation: 关于UBI的实施存在什么争议？

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage Two
readingPassages.content:
The decline of biodiversity is one of the most critical environmental challenges of our era, yet it receives considerably less attention than climate change. Scientists warn that we are experiencing the sixth mass extinction event in Earths history, with species disappearing at a rate 100 to 1,000 times higher than the natural background rate. Unlike previous mass extinctions caused by natural phenomena, this one is overwhelmingly driven by human activities.

The primary drivers of biodiversity loss are well-documented and interconnected. Habitat destruction, largely through deforestation, urbanization, and agricultural expansion, is the single greatest threat to species. The conversion of natural ecosystems into farmland has reduced the habitats of countless species, particularly in tropical regions that harbor the majority of the worlds biodiversity. Climate change compounds this problem by altering the environmental conditions that species have adapted to over millennia, forcing them to migrate, adapt, or face extinction.

Overexploitation of natural resources is another major factor. Unsustainable fishing practices have pushed many marine species to the brink of collapse. The International Union for Conservation of Nature reports that over one-third of shark and ray species are threatened with extinction primarily due to overfishing. Similarly, illegal wildlife trade threatens thousands of species, from pangolins to elephants, driving many toward extinction for commercial gain.

The consequences of biodiversity loss extend far beyond the disappearance of individual species. Ecosystems provide essential services that underpin human civilization, including pollination of crops, purification of water, regulation of climate, and disease control. The economic value of these ecosystem services is estimated to be in the tens of trillions of dollars annually. When biodiversity declines, the resilience of these systems is compromised, making them more vulnerable to shocks and less able to recover from disturbances.

Conservation efforts have achieved some notable successes. The recovery of species such as the giant panda and the humpback whale demonstrates that targeted conservation interventions can work. Protected areas now cover approximately 15 percent of the Earths land surface, though coverage of marine environments remains inadequate. The Convention on Biological Diversity has established targets for biodiversity conservation, though progress toward these targets has been uneven. Effective conservation requires not only protected areas but also addressing the underlying economic and social drivers of biodiversity loss, including unsustainable consumption patterns and inequitable resource distribution.

questions.questionText: How does the current mass extinction differ from previous ones?

B. It is primarily caused by human activities.

questions.translation: 多少比例的鲨鱼和鳐鱼物种面临灭绝威胁？

options: A. Only establishing more protected areas.

translation.chineseText: 中国高速铁路的发展成就是世界瞩目的。截至2024年，中国高铁运营里程已超过4.5万公里，位居世界第一。高铁极大地缩短了城市之间的时空距离，改变了人们的生活方式和经济格局。从北京到上海，原来需要十几个小时的旅程，现在高铁仅需四个半小时。高铁网络连接了全国大部分主要城市，促进了区域经济一体化发展。中国高铁技术已经走出国门，在印度尼西亚、老挝等国家承建的高铁项目已成为一带一路倡议的亮丽名片。
translation.englishAnswer: The development achievements of Chinas high-speed railway have attracted worldwide attention. As of 2024, the operating mileage of Chinas high-speed railway has exceeded 45,000 kilometers, ranking first in the world. High-speed rail has greatly shortened the spatial and temporal distance between cities, transforming peoples lifestyles and the economic landscape. The journey from Beijing to Shanghai, which used to take more than ten hours, now takes only four and a half hours by high-speed rail. The high-speed rail network connects most major cities across the country, promoting regional economic integration. Chinese high-speed rail technology has gone global, with projects undertaken in countries such as Indonesia and Laos serving as shining examples of the Belt and Road Initiative.
keyPoints: 词汇：operating mileage, spatial and temporal distance, economic integration
六级复杂句式：which used to take...（定语从句）
专有名词：Belt and Road Initiative（一带一路倡议）
数据表达：exceeded 45,000 kilometers
translation.explanation: 本题主题为中国高铁成就，是六级翻译中"社会发展"类典型题目。注意：1) "运营里程"译为operating mileage；2) "时空距离"译为spatial and temporal distance；3) "一带一路"为专有名词，译为the Belt and Road Initiative；4) 注意数据表达的准确性。

listeningSections.title: Conversation One
listeningSections.script:
W: Welcome to our podcast on sustainable transportation. Today we have Dr. Mark Chen, an urban mobility expert. Dr. Chen, what are the biggest challenges facing urban transportation today?
M: Thank you for having me. The biggest challenge is undoubtedly the over-reliance on private vehicles. This leads to traffic congestion, air pollution, and carbon emissions. Many cities were designed around cars, and now we're dealing with the consequences.
W: What solutions are cities implementing?
M: There's a growing trend toward what we call "complete streets" — roads designed for all users, not just cars. This means dedicated bike lanes, wider sidewalks, and efficient public transit. Copenhagen is a great example, where over 60% of residents commute by bicycle.
W: What about electric vehicles? Are they the answer?
M: EVs are part of the solution, but not a complete one. They address emissions but don't solve congestion or space issues. A truly sustainable transportation system combines electrification with reduced car dependency. Shared mobility services, when properly integrated with public transit, can be very effective.
W: How can cities fund these transportation improvements?
M: Innovative funding mechanisms include congestion pricing, as implemented in London and Singapore. Parking fees can also be structured to encourage alternatives. Many cities are redirecting a portion of fuel taxes to public transit and cycling infrastructure.
W: What role does technology play?
M: Technology enables smart traffic management, real-time transit information, and integrated payment systems. However, technology should serve the goal of moving people efficiently, not just collecting data. The human element remains central.

questions.explanation: 陈博士指出最大的挑战是过度依赖私家车，导致交通拥堵、空气污染和碳排放。
options: A. Lack of public transit funding
B. Over-reliance on private vehicles

options: A. Streets with no traffic lights
B. Roads designed for all users, not just cars
C. Highways connecting all city districts

options: A. They are too expensive

listeningSections.title: Conversation Two
listeningSections.script:
M: Professor Williams, your research on sleep science has gained a lot of attention. Could you share some key findings?
W: Certainly. One of the most important discoveries is that sleep is not a passive state but an active process essential for health. During sleep, the brain clears out waste products, consolidates memories, and regulates emotions.
M: How much sleep do people actually need?
W: The National Sleep Foundation recommends 7-9 hours for adults. However, individual needs vary. The key indicator is how you feel during the day. If you need caffeine to stay alert or feel sleepy during meetings, you're probably not getting enough sleep.
M: What about the impact of technology on sleep?
W: Blue light from screens suppresses melatonin production, the hormone that regulates sleep. I recommend avoiding screens for at least an hour before bed. Also, the constant stimulation from social media can make it difficult for the mind to wind down.
M: Are there effective treatments for insomnia?
W: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia, or CBT-I, is the most effective treatment. It addresses the thoughts and behaviors that interfere with sleep. Unlike sleep medications, CBT-I has no side effects and provides lasting benefits. Sleep hygiene practices, such as maintaining a consistent schedule and creating a cool, dark sleeping environment, are also important.

questions.translation: 教授对睡眠有什么看法？
questions.explanation: 教授指出睡眠不是被动状态，而是对健康至关重要的主动过程，大脑在睡眠中清除废物、巩固记忆和调节情绪。
options: A. It is a passive state
B. It is an active process essential for health
C. It is only important for children
D. It is not necessary for adults

options: A. 5-6 hours

D. 9-10 hours

D. It only affects children's sleep

listeningSections.title: Passage One
listeningSections.script:
The sharing economy has fundamentally changed how people access goods and services. From ride-sharing to home-sharing, platform-based markets have created new opportunities for individuals to monetize their assets and skills. However, the rapid growth of these platforms has also raised important questions about regulation, labor rights, and the long-term economic impact.

Proponents of the sharing economy argue that it increases efficiency by utilizing underused assets. A car that sits parked 95% of the time can be put to work through ride-sharing services. A spare bedroom can generate income through home-sharing platforms. This asset utilization reduces waste and can lower costs for consumers. Furthermore, these platforms have created flexible earning opportunities for millions of people worldwide.

Critics, however, point to significant concerns. Many sharing economy platforms classify workers as independent contractors rather than employees, denying them benefits such as health insurance, paid leave, and retirement contributions. There are also concerns about these platforms contributing to housing shortages in popular tourist destinations and undermining established industries such as hotels and taxis.

The regulatory response has varied widely. Some cities have imposed strict limits on short-term rentals, while others have embraced the sharing economy as a source of innovation. The challenge for policymakers is to strike a balance between encouraging innovation and protecting consumers and workers. As the sharing economy continues to evolve, finding this balance will remain a critical task for governments around the world.

questions.translation: 共享经济支持者的主要论点是什么？
questions.explanation: 支持者认为共享经济通过利用未充分利用的资产来提高效率。
options: A. It eliminates all traditional businesses
B. It increases efficiency by utilizing underused assets
C. It only benefits large corporations
D. It reduces the need for regulation
questions.questionText: What is the main argument of sharing economy proponents?

options: A. Workers are overpaid
B. Workers are classified as independent contractors without benefits
C. Workers have too much flexibility

options: A. All cities have banned the sharing economy
B. Some cities impose limits while others embrace it
C. No cities have any regulations

options: A. Banning all sharing platforms
B. Striking a balance between innovation and protection
C. Taxing all transactions
D. Privatizing all platforms

listeningSections.title: Passage Two
listeningSections.script:
The field of positive psychology, founded by Martin Seligman in the late 1990s, shifted the focus of psychology from treating mental illness to promoting human flourishing. Rather than asking what goes wrong with people, positive psychology asks what makes life worth living and how individuals and communities can thrive.

One of the central concepts in positive psychology is the PERMA model, which identifies five elements essential for well-being: Positive emotions, Engagement, Relationships, Meaning, and Accomplishment. According to Seligman, these five pillars are not merely routes to happiness but are pursued for their own sake and contribute to a fulfilling life.

Research in positive psychology has produced practical interventions that increase well-being. The "three good things" exercise, where individuals write down three positive events each day and reflect on why they happened, has been shown to significantly increase happiness and decrease depressive symptoms for up to six months. Similarly, using signature strengths in new ways each week has been found to boost engagement and life satisfaction.

Positive psychology has been applied in various domains, including education, where programs teaching resilience and optimism have reduced depression rates among students; in the workplace, where focusing on employee strengths rather than weaknesses has increased productivity; and in clinical settings, where positive psychotherapy has proven effective for treating depression. The field continues to grow, with researchers exploring topics such as gratitude, mindfulness, and the psychology of meaningful work.

questions.translation: 积极心理学的关注点是什么？
questions.explanation: 积极心理学将关注点从治疗精神疾病转向促进人类繁荣发展。
options: A. Treating mental illness
B. Promoting human flourishing
C. Studying brain chemistry
D. Analyzing childhood trauma

options: A. Five types of mental disorders
B. Five elements essential for well-being
C. Five stages of child development

options: A. It increases anxiety

readingPassages.title: Section A - 选词填空
readingPassages.content: The field of epigenetics has revolutionized our understanding of how genes and environment interact. Epigenetics refers to changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the DNA sequence itself. These changes can be (1)______ by environmental factors such as diet, stress, and exposure to toxins, and in some cases, they can even be passed down to future generations. This discovery has (2)______ implications for our understanding of health and disease. Traditional genetics held that our DNA sequence is fixed and largely (3)______ our destiny. Epigenetics reveals a more dynamic picture, in which our genes are constantly responding to our environment. This helps explain why identical twins, who share the same DNA, can develop different diseases as they age. Their epigenetic marks (4)______ over time due to different life experiences. One of the most well-studied examples of epigenetics involves the Dutch Hunger Winter of 1944-1945. Children who were conceived during this period of severe famine were found to have higher rates of obesity, heart disease, and mental illness later in life. Epigenetic changes (5)______ by their mothers malnutrition had been passed to them, affecting their metabolism and health decades later. The (6)______ of epigenetic research for medicine is enormous. Drugs that can reverse harmful epigenetic changes are already being developed and used in cancer treatment. Unlike changes to the DNA sequence itself, epigenetic changes are potentially (7)______, opening up new possibilities for therapy. Researchers are also exploring how lifestyle interventions such as diet and exercise might (8)______ positive epigenetic changes. However, the field also raises ethical questions. If our experiences can (9)______ our descendants, what responsibilities do we have to future generations? The ability to edit epigenetic marks also raises concerns about the (10)______ for misuse. As with any powerful technology, the benefits of epigenetic knowledge must be weighed against its potential risks.

questions.questionText: changes can be (1)______ by environmental factors
questions.explanation: influenced意为"影响"，表观遗传变化受饮食、压力和环境毒素等环境因素影响。
questions.translation: 这些变化可能受到饮食、压力和毒素暴露等环境因素的影响。

questions.questionText: has (2)______ implications
questions.explanation: profound意为"深远的"，表观遗传学的发现对健康和疾病的理解具有深远影响。
questions.translation: 这一发现对我们理解健康和疾病具有深远意义。

questions.questionText: largely (3)______ our destiny
questions.explanation: determines意为"决定"，传统遗传学认为DNA序列是固定的，在很大程度上决定了我们的命运。
questions.translation: 传统遗传学认为我们的DNA序列是固定的，在很大程度上决定了我们的命运。

questions.questionText: epigenetic marks (4)______ over time
questions.explanation: diverge意为"分歧、分化"，同卵双胞胎的表观遗传标记随着时间推移因不同生活经历而产生差异。
questions.translation: 他们的表观遗传标记因不同的生活经历而随着时间的推移产生差异。

questions.questionText: Epigenetic changes (5)______ by their mothers malnutrition
questions.explanation: induced意为"诱发、引起"，母亲营养不良诱发的表观遗传变化被传递给子女。
questions.translation: 由母亲营养不良诱发的表观遗传变化被传递给了他们。

questions.questionText: The (6)______ of epigenetic research for medicine
questions.explanation: promise意为"前景、潜力"，表观遗传学研究在医学领域的潜力巨大。
questions.translation: 表观遗传学研究在医学领域的前景巨大。

questions.questionText: epigenetic changes are potentially (7)______
questions.explanation: reversible意为"可逆的"，表观遗传变化与DNA序列变化不同，是潜在可逆的。
questions.translation: 与DNA序列本身的变化不同，表观遗传变化是潜在可逆的。

questions.questionText: might (8)______ positive epigenetic changes
questions.explanation: promote意为"促进"，研究人员正在探索饮食和运动等生活方式干预如何促进积极的表观遗传变化。
questions.translation: 研究人员正在探索饮食和运动等生活方式干预如何促进积极的表观遗传变化。

questions.questionText: experiences can (9)______ our descendants
questions.explanation: affect意为"影响"，我们的经历会影响后代，引发伦理问题。
questions.translation: 如果我们的经历会影响后代，我们对未来世代负有什么责任？

questions.questionText: concerns about the (10)______ for misuse
questions.explanation: potential意为"可能性、潜在性"，编辑表观遗传标记的能力引发了对滥用可能性的担忧。
questions.translation: 编辑表观遗传标记的能力也引发了对滥用可能性的担忧。

readingPassages.title: Section B - 长篇阅读匹配
readingPassages.content:
A) The global food system is at a crossroads. Current methods of food production are degrading the natural resources on which agriculture depends, while simultaneously contributing to climate change, biodiversity loss, and water scarcity. At the same time, nearly 800 million people worldwide suffer from chronic hunger, while over 2 billion are overweight or obese. These paradoxes have led to growing calls for a fundamental transformation of how we produce, distribute, and consume food.

B) Industrial agriculture, which has dominated food production since the Green Revolution, has been remarkably successful at increasing yields. Global food production has more than tripled since 1960, enabling the world to feed a growing population. However, these gains have come at a substantial environmental cost. Industrial agriculture is responsible for approximately 30 percent of global greenhouse gas emissions, consumes 70 percent of freshwater resources, and is a major driver of deforestation and biodiversity loss.

C) Food waste is another critical issue in the global food system. Approximately one-third of all food produced for human consumption is lost or wasted each year, amounting to about 1.3 billion tons. In developing countries, most food loss occurs early in the supply chain due to inadequate storage, transportation, and refrigeration infrastructure. In developed countries, food waste is concentrated at the retail and consumer levels, driven by cosmetic standards for produce, confusing date labels, and consumer behavior.

D) Sustainable agriculture offers an alternative paradigm. Practices such as agroecology, regenerative agriculture, and precision farming aim to produce food while minimizing environmental impact. These approaches emphasize soil health, water conservation, and biodiversity. Techniques such as crop rotation, cover cropping, and integrated pest management can reduce the need for synthetic fertilizers and pesticides. Studies have shown that sustainable agricultural practices can be as productive as conventional methods in the long term, while providing greater resilience to climate shocks.

E) Plant-based and alternative proteins are gaining traction as a strategy to reduce the environmental footprint of food production. The livestock sector is a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions and land use, and reducing meat consumption is widely recognized as one of the most effective individual actions for reducing ones environmental impact. The market for plant-based meat alternatives has grown rapidly, and cultivated meat produced from animal cells without slaughter is approaching commercial viability.

F) Technology is playing an increasingly important role in transforming the food system. Precision agriculture uses sensors, drones, and artificial intelligence to optimize inputs such as water, fertilizer, and pesticides, reducing waste and environmental impact. Vertical farming allows year-round production of fresh produce in urban areas, reducing transportation distances. Blockchain technology is being used to improve traceability and transparency in food supply chains, enabling consumers to verify the origin and sustainability of their food.

G) Policy interventions are crucial for enabling a transition to a sustainable food system. Agricultural subsidies, which currently amount to over $600 billion annually worldwide, often incentivize unsustainable practices. Redirecting these subsidies toward sustainable agriculture could accelerate the adoption of environmentally friendly farming methods. Carbon pricing for agriculture, taxes on foods with high environmental impact, and procurement policies that prioritize sustainable food are among the policy options being discussed.

H) Dietary shifts are an essential component of food system transformation. The EAT-Lancet Commission on healthy diets from sustainable food systems has proposed a planetary health diet that emphasizes plant-based foods and limits red meat, sugar, and refined grains. Adopting such a diet globally could prevent approximately 11 million premature deaths per year while reducing the environmental impact of food production. However, dietary change is deeply personal and culturally embedded, making it challenging to promote through policy alone.

I) Smallholder farmers, who produce a significant portion of the worlds food, must be central to any transformation of the food system. These farmers, many of whom are women, often lack access to credit, technology, and markets. Empowering smallholder farmers through secure land rights, access to finance, and training in sustainable practices is essential for both food security and environmental sustainability.

J) Transforming the global food system will not be easy, but the costs of inaction are far greater than the costs of change. A sustainable food system would not only reduce environmental degradation but also improve public health, enhance food security, and create economic opportunities. The challenge lies in navigating the complex trade-offs between different goals and ensuring that the transition is just and inclusive for all stakeholders, from farmers to consumers.

questions.questionText: Industrial agriculture has increased yields but at a substantial environmental cost.
questions.explanation: 段落B指出工业农业自绿色革命以来显著提高了产量，但付出了巨大的环境代价。
questions.translation: 工业农业提高了产量，但付出了巨大的环境代价。

questions.questionText: Precision agriculture uses technology to optimize inputs like water and fertilizers.
questions.explanation: 段落F指出精准农业使用传感器、无人机和人工智能来优化水、肥料和农药等投入。
questions.translation: 精准农业使用技术来优化水和肥料等投入。

questions.questionText: Agricultural subsidies often incentivize unsustainable farming practices.
questions.explanation: 段落G指出全球每年超过6000亿美元的农业补贴往往激励不可持续的做法。
questions.translation: 农业补贴往往激励不可持续的农业实践。

questions.questionText: The planetary health diet could prevent millions of premature deaths annually.
questions.explanation: 段落H指出采用地球健康饮食每年可以防止约1100万过早死亡。
questions.translation: 地球健康饮食每年可以防止数百万过早死亡。

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage One
readingPassages.content:
The nature of creativity has long fascinated psychologists, neuroscientists, and philosophers. Recent advances in neuroimaging have allowed researchers to observe the brain in action during creative tasks, revealing that creativity is not the product of a single brain region or hemisphere but rather emerges from the dynamic interaction of large-scale brain networks.

One of the most influential models of creativity is the dual-process model, which distinguishes between divergent and convergent thinking. Divergent thinking involves generating multiple ideas or solutions to a problem, characterized by free association and broad exploration. Convergent thinking, on the other hand, involves narrowing down possibilities to identify the single best solution. Both modes are essential for creativity, and creative individuals are those who can flexibly switch between them as the task demands.

Research has identified three key brain networks involved in creative thinking. The default mode network, which is active when the mind is at rest and wandering, is associated with idea generation and imagination. The executive control network, which is involved in focused attention and cognitive control, helps evaluate and refine ideas. The salience network acts as a switch, detecting which ideas are relevant and directing attention accordingly. Effective creativity involves the dynamic interplay of these networks, with the brain cycling between periods of spontaneous generation and deliberate evaluation.

Environmental factors play a crucial role in fostering or inhibiting creativity. Contrary to the romantic image of the solitary genius, creativity is often a collaborative process. Research shows that diverse teams, where members bring different perspectives and expertise, tend to be more creative than homogeneous groups. Psychological safety, or the belief that one can take risks without fear of negative consequences, is essential for creative collaboration. Physical environment also matters, with factors such as natural light, access to nature, and opportunities for informal interaction influencing creative output.

The relationship between creativity and mental health is complex and often misunderstood. While certain mood disorders have been associated with creative achievement, the vast majority of creative individuals do not suffer from mental illness. The notion of the tortured artist is largely a myth. However, certain personality traits associated with creativity, such as openness to experience and sensitivity to the environment, may also confer vulnerability to certain mental health challenges. Understanding this relationship requires nuanced research that avoids both romanticizing mental illness and stigmatizing creative individuals.

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage Two
readingPassages.content:
The governance of artificial intelligence has become one of the most pressing policy challenges of our time. As AI systems become more capable and pervasive, questions about how to ensure they are developed and deployed responsibly have moved from academic circles to the highest levels of government and corporate decision-making. The challenge lies in harnessing the benefits of AI while mitigating its risks, a balancing act that different jurisdictions are approaching in markedly different ways.

The European Union has taken a rights-based approach to AI governance. Its proposed AI Act categorizes AI applications according to their level of risk, with different regulatory requirements for each category. Applications deemed to pose unacceptable risks, such as social scoring systems by governments and real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces, would be banned entirely. High-risk applications, including those used in critical infrastructure, education, employment, and law enforcement, would be subject to strict requirements for transparency, accountability, and human oversight. This approach reflects the EUs emphasis on fundamental rights and its willingness to restrict certain uses of AI to protect them.

In contrast, the United States has adopted a more sectoral and industry-led approach. While the Biden administration has issued an Executive Order on Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy AI, the US approach relies more on voluntary commitments from companies and existing regulatory agencies than on comprehensive new legislation. Major AI companies have made voluntary commitments to safety testing, watermarking of AI-generated content, and transparency about capabilities and limitations. Critics argue that this approach may be insufficient to address the systemic risks posed by advanced AI systems.

China has pursued a state-centric approach that emphasizes technological development and national security. The country has issued regulations on algorithmic recommendations, deep synthesis, and generative AI, requiring companies to register their algorithms and ensure that AI-generated content aligns with core socialist values. At the same time, the Chinese government has made AI development a national priority, investing heavily in AI research and infrastructure. This dual focus on development and control reflects Chinas broader approach to technology governance.

Despite these different approaches, there is growing recognition of the need for international coordination on AI governance. AI systems operate across borders, and regulatory fragmentation could create challenges for both innovation and safety. International forums such as the Global Partnership on AI and the OECD have begun developing principles for responsible AI, but translating these principles into binding agreements remains a significant challenge. The speed of AI development continues to outpace the speed of governance, creating a persistent gap between technological capabilities and regulatory frameworks.

translation.chineseText: 中国航天事业取得了举世瞩目的成就。从第一颗人造卫星东方红一号到载人航天工程，从嫦娥探月工程到天宫空间站，中国航天人一代代不懈努力，实现了从无到有、从弱到强的跨越式发展。2023年，中国空间站全面建成，标志着中国航天事业进入了新的阶段。航天技术的发展不仅推动了科技进步，还在通信、导航、气象观测等领域造福了人民生活。中国积极开展航天国际合作，与多个国家共同探索宇宙的奥秘。
translation.englishAnswer: China's space industry has achieved remarkable accomplishments that have attracted worldwide attention. From the first artificial satellite Dongfanghong-1 to the manned space program, from the Chang'e lunar exploration project to the Tiangong space station, generations of Chinese space scientists have made unremitting efforts, realizing a leapfrog development from scratch and from weakness to strength. In 2023, the China Space Station was fully completed, marking a new stage in China's space industry. The development of space technology has not only promoted scientific and technological progress but also benefited people's lives in areas such as communications, navigation, and meteorological observation. China actively carries out international cooperation in space, exploring the mysteries of the universe together with multiple countries.
keyPoints: 词汇：artificial satellite, manned space program, lunar exploration, leapfrog development
专有名词：Dongfanghong-1（东方红一号）, Chang'e（嫦娥）, Tiangong（天宫）
六级句式：from...to...（从……到……的排比结构）
被动语态：was fully completed
translation.explanation: 本题主题为中国航天成就。注意：1) "东方红一号"是专有名词，译为Dongfanghong-1；2) "嫦娥"译为Chang'e；3) "天宫"译为Tiangong；4) "跨越式发展"译为leapfrog development；5) 注意航天领域专业术语的准确性。

listeningSections.title: Conversation One
listeningSections.script:
W: Dr. Patel, thank you for joining us to discuss the future of renewable energy. What recent developments are most promising?
M: I'm glad to be here. The most exciting developments are in solar energy efficiency and battery storage technology. Solar panel efficiency has increased dramatically, with some commercial panels now converting over 25% of sunlight into electricity. Meanwhile, battery costs have fallen by nearly 90% over the past decade.
W: How does this compare to traditional energy sources?
M: In many markets, renewable energy is now cheaper than coal and natural gas. This is a game-changer. The economic argument for renewables has never been stronger. However, intermittency remains a challenge — the sun doesn't always shine, and the wind doesn't always blow.
W: How are we addressing the intermittency problem?
M: Grid-scale battery storage is one solution. Another is geographic diversification — connecting wind farms in different regions so that when the wind isn't blowing in one place, it is in another. Smart grid technology also helps by managing demand and supply more effectively.
W: What role does government policy play?
M: Policy has been crucial. Feed-in tariffs, tax incentives, and renewable portfolio standards have accelerated adoption. The Inflation Reduction Act in the United States is expected to significantly boost renewable energy investment. International cooperation, like the Paris Agreement, provides the framework for global action.
W: What about nuclear energy? Is it making a comeback?
M: There's growing interest in advanced nuclear technologies, including small modular reactors. Some see nuclear as a complement to renewables because it provides consistent baseload power. However, concerns about waste disposal and cost remain significant barriers.

listeningSections.title: Conversation Two
listeningSections.script:
M: Professor Wang, your work on ocean plastic pollution has been widely recognized. Can you tell us about the scale of the problem?
W: The scale is truly alarming. Approximately 8 million tons of plastic enter the ocean each year. That's equivalent to dumping a garbage truck full of plastic into the ocean every minute. If current trends continue, there could be more plastic than fish in the ocean by 2050.
M: Where does all this plastic come from?
W: About 80% of ocean plastic comes from land-based sources. Rivers are major pathways, with just ten rivers, mostly in Asia and Africa, carrying 90% of the plastic that reaches the ocean. Single-use plastics, such as packaging, bottles, and bags, make up a significant portion.
M: What are the effects on marine life?
W: The effects are devastating. Over 800 species are affected by marine plastic pollution through ingestion and entanglement. Microplastics have been found in the deepest parts of the ocean and in Arctic ice. They've also entered the food chain and have been detected in human blood and organs.
M: What solutions are being implemented?
W: Solutions range from international agreements to local initiatives. The United Nations is working toward a global plastic treaty. Many countries have banned single-use plastics. Technological solutions include ocean cleanup systems and biodegradable materials. However, the most effective approach is reducing plastic production and improving waste management infrastructure.

listeningSections.title: Passage One
listeningSections.script:
Emotional intelligence, often referred to as EQ, has become a widely discussed concept in psychology and business. First popularized by psychologist Daniel Goleman in the 1990s, emotional intelligence refers to the ability to recognize, understand, and manage our own emotions, as well as to recognize, understand, and influence the emotions of others.

Goleman's model identifies four key components of emotional intelligence. Self-awareness is the foundation — the ability to recognize and understand your own emotions as they occur. Self-management involves controlling impulsive feelings and behaviors, managing emotions in healthy ways, and adapting to changing circumstances. Social awareness, particularly empathy, is the ability to understand the emotional makeup of other people and treat them according to their emotional reactions. Relationship management involves the ability to develop and maintain good relationships, communicate clearly, inspire and influence others, and manage conflict.

Research has shown that emotional intelligence is a strong predictor of success in many areas of life. Studies have found that EQ is a better predictor of workplace success than IQ in many professions, particularly those involving leadership and teamwork. Individuals with high emotional intelligence tend to have better mental health, more satisfying relationships, and higher job performance.

Unlike IQ, which remains relatively stable throughout life, emotional intelligence can be developed and improved with practice. Training programs that focus on mindfulness, active listening, and perspective-taking have been shown to increase emotional intelligence. As workplaces become more collaborative and diverse, emotional intelligence is likely to become an increasingly valuable skill.
