listeningSections.title: Passage Two
listeningSections.script:
The rise of remote work has been one of the most significant transformations in the modern workplace. What began as a necessity during the pandemic has become a preferred mode of work for millions of employees worldwide. Companies that once resisted flexible work arrangements have now adopted hybrid models as a permanent feature of their operations.

Research on remote work productivity has yielded interesting results. A comprehensive study by Stanford University found that remote workers were 13% more productive than their office-based counterparts, with the increase attributed to a quieter, more focused work environment and fewer breaks and sick days. However, the same study noted that collaboration and innovation, measured by the exchange of ideas across teams, showed some decline in fully remote settings.

The impact on employee well-being has been mixed. Many workers report improved work-life balance, reduced commuting stress, and greater autonomy over their schedules. However, others struggle with feelings of isolation, difficulty separating work from personal life, and reduced opportunities for mentorship and social connection. The blurring of boundaries between work and home has led to concerns about burnout.

Companies have responded by investing in digital collaboration tools, rethinking performance evaluation methods, and developing new norms for remote communication. Successful hybrid models typically include regular in-person gatherings for team building and strategic planning, while allowing flexibility for individual work. As technology continues to evolve, the future of work will likely involve even more flexible arrangements, with organizations competing for talent based on the quality of their remote work offerings.

readingPassages.title: Section A - 选词填空
readingPassages.content: The concept of corporate social responsibility has evolved significantly over the past several decades. What was once viewed as a peripheral concern for businesses has become a central strategic consideration. Companies are increasingly expected to (1)______ their operations with broader societal values and to be accountable not only to shareholders but also to a wider range of stakeholders, including employees, customers, communities, and the environment. This shift has been driven by multiple factors. Growing (2)______ of environmental issues, particularly climate change, has put pressure on companies to reduce their carbon footprint and adopt more sustainable practices. The rise of social media has (3)______ corporate behavior, making it easier for activists and consumers to expose unethical practices and mobilize campaigns against companies that fall short of public expectations. Institutional investors are increasingly incorporating environmental, social, and governance criteria into their investment decisions, (4)______ companies to improve their performance on these dimensions. However, the implementation of CSR is not without challenges. Critics (5)______ that many companies engage in greenwashing, making misleading claims about their environmental efforts to improve their public image without making (6)______ changes. There is also tension between the demands of CSR and the pressure to deliver short-term financial results. Companies that invest heavily in CSR may find themselves at a (7)______ disadvantage compared to competitors who do not. Despite these challenges, there is evidence that CSR can create long-term value. Companies with strong CSR (8)______ often enjoy better brand reputation, higher employee morale, and stronger customer loyalty. A growing body of research suggests that such companies also (9)______ their competitors financially over the long term. The key is to integrate CSR into core business strategy rather than treating it as a separate (10)______ or public relations exercise. When done authentically, CSR can be a source of competitive advantage rather than a cost to be minimized.

questions.questionText: expected to (1)______ their operations with broader societal values

readingPassages.title: Section B - 长篇阅读匹配
readingPassages.content:
A) Urbanization has been one of the defining trends of the twenty-first century. For the first time in human history, more people live in cities than in rural areas, and this proportion continues to grow. While cities have historically been engines of economic growth and innovation, they also face enormous challenges, from housing affordability to environmental sustainability. How cities manage these challenges will shape the quality of life for billions of people in the coming decades.

B) Housing affordability has become a crisis in many of the worlds major cities. In cities like San Francisco, London, and Hong Kong, the ratio of median house prices to median incomes has reached levels that make homeownership unattainable for a large portion of the population. This crisis has been driven by a combination of factors, including constrained housing supply due to zoning regulations and land-use restrictions, rising construction costs, and the financialization of housing, where properties are treated as investment assets rather than places to live.

C) The consequences of the housing affordability crisis extend far beyond the housing market itself. Workers in essential but low-paid professions, such as teachers, nurses, and firefighters, are being priced out of the cities they serve, leading to labor shortages and longer commutes. Young people are delaying marriage and family formation due to the inability to afford housing. The crisis also contributes to homelessness and housing insecurity, with significant social and economic costs.

D) Various policy solutions have been proposed to address housing affordability. Inclusionary zoning policies require developers to include affordable units in new developments. Rent control measures aim to protect tenants from excessive rent increases, though economists are divided on their effectiveness. Some cities have experimented with vacancy taxes to discourage property speculation. Increasing housing density through upzoning and allowing taller buildings in central locations is another approach, though it often faces opposition from existing residents concerned about neighborhood character.

E) Transportation is another critical challenge for cities. Traffic congestion imposes enormous economic costs through lost productivity and wasted fuel. Many cities are investing in public transit systems, bike lanes, and pedestrian infrastructure to provide alternatives to car travel. The concept of the 15-minute city, where residents can meet most of their daily needs within a 15-minute walk or bike ride from their homes, has gained popularity as a model for urban planning that reduces car dependence and improves quality of life.

F) Climate change presents existential challenges for cities, particularly coastal cities threatened by sea-level rise. Cities are both major contributors to climate change, accounting for over 70 percent of global carbon emissions, and particularly vulnerable to its impacts. Many cities have taken ambitious climate action, setting targets for carbon neutrality and implementing measures such as building efficiency standards, renewable energy mandates, and green infrastructure. Urban green spaces, from parks to green roofs, play a crucial role in both mitigation and adaptation.

G) Technology is transforming urban life in profound ways. Smart city technologies, from intelligent traffic management to smart grids, promise to make cities more efficient and sustainable. Digital platforms have reshaped how people work, shop, and socialize in cities. However, technology also poses risks, including the digital divide that excludes those without access to digital tools and the potential for surveillance technologies to infringe on privacy rights.

H) Social inequality is a persistent challenge in cities. While cities generate enormous wealth, this wealth is often unevenly distributed. Spatial inequality, where wealthy and poor neighborhoods are physically separated, has increased in many cities. This segregation perpetuates inequality by concentrating disadvantage and limiting access to opportunities. Addressing urban inequality requires comprehensive strategies that encompass housing, education, employment, and social services.

I) Citizen participation in urban governance is essential for creating cities that serve the needs of all residents. Participatory budgeting, where residents have a direct say in how public funds are spent, has been implemented in cities around the world. Community planning processes that involve residents in decisions about neighborhood development can lead to outcomes that better reflect local needs and priorities. However, ensuring meaningful participation requires overcoming barriers such as time constraints, language barriers, and unequal access to information.

J) The future of cities will be shaped by the choices we make today. Climate change, demographic shifts, and technological change will all profoundly influence urban development. The most successful cities will be those that can adapt to these changes while ensuring that the benefits of urban life are shared equitably. This requires visionary leadership, sustained investment, and a commitment to inclusive decision-making processes that give all residents a stake in the future of their communities.

questions.questionText: Housing affordability is driven by factors including constrained supply and the financialization of housing.
questions.explanation: 段落B指出住房可负担性危机由多种因素驱动，包括分区法规导致的供应受限和住房金融化。

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage One
readingPassages.content:
The concept of the attention economy has become increasingly relevant in understanding the dynamics of the digital age. In this framework, human attention is understood as a scarce resource that technology companies compete for. The business models of major digital platforms are built on capturing and monetizing user attention, primarily through advertising. This fundamental economic reality shapes everything from how platforms are designed to the kinds of content that are promoted.

Tristan Harris, a former Google design ethicist and co-founder of the Center for Humane Technology, has been a prominent critic of how the attention economy operates. He argues that technology companies have engaged in a race to the bottom, designing products that exploit psychological vulnerabilities to maximize user engagement. Features such as infinite scroll, push notifications, and autoplay are not neutral design choices but deliberate mechanisms to capture and hold attention. Harris compares this to a slot machine, where variable rewards create addictive patterns of behavior.

The consequences of this attention-extraction model are increasingly well-documented. Studies have linked heavy social media use to decreased wellbeing, particularly among adolescents. The constant interruption of notifications fragments attention and reduces the ability to focus deeply on complex tasks. The spread of misinformation is facilitated by algorithms that prioritize emotionally engaging content over accurate content, as emotional content tends to capture more attention. Political polarization is amplified by recommendation systems that feed users increasingly extreme content to keep them engaged.

Regulatory responses to the attention economy have begun to emerge. The European Unions Digital Services Act requires platforms to assess and mitigate systemic risks, including risks to mental health and civic discourse. Some jurisdictions have considered banning certain addictive design features, such as infinite scroll and autoplay. Legislation aimed at protecting children from harmful online content has been introduced in several countries. However, regulating the attention economy is complex, as it involves fundamental questions about free speech, technological neutrality, and personal responsibility.

Proponents of reform argue for a fundamental redesign of digital platforms, moving from an attention-extraction model to one that prioritizes human wellbeing. This could include design features that respect user attention, such as default limits on screen time, algorithms that prioritize quality over engagement, and business models that do not rely on advertising. The challenge is that such changes would require technology companies to voluntarily reduce their revenues or face regulation that mandates such changes. As awareness of the attention economy grows, the pressure on platforms to reform is likely to intensify.

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage Two
readingPassages.content:
The phenomenon of global migration has become one of the most politically charged issues of our time. Yet beneath the heated political rhetoric lies a complex reality that is often poorly understood. Migration is driven by a combination of push factors, such as conflict, persecution, and economic hardship, and pull factors, such as labor demand, family reunification, and educational opportunities. Understanding these dynamics is essential for developing effective and humane migration policies.

Economic migration represents the largest category of international migration. Workers move from lower-wage to higher-wage countries, filling both high-skilled positions in technology and healthcare and low-skilled positions in agriculture, construction, and domestic work. The economic benefits of migration are substantial. Immigrants contribute to economic growth, innovation, and entrepreneurship in destination countries. A study by the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine found that immigration has an overall positive impact on long-run economic growth in the United States. Remittances sent by migrants to their home countries also provide a vital source of income for millions of families.

Forced migration, resulting from conflict, persecution, and natural disasters, presents distinct challenges. The number of forcibly displaced people worldwide has reached record levels, exceeding 100 million for the first time in 2022. Refugees, who flee persecution and cannot safely return home, are entitled to international protection under the 1951 Refugee Convention. Asylum seekers must navigate complex legal processes to have their claims recognized. The distinction between economic migrants and refugees is not always clear-cut, as people often have mixed motives for moving.

Integration of migrants into host societies is a critical challenge that determines the outcomes of migration for both newcomers and receiving communities. Successful integration involves multiple dimensions, including economic integration through employment, social integration through language acquisition and community participation, and civic integration through access to rights and citizenship. Countries with comprehensive integration policies tend to have better outcomes for both migrants and native-born populations.

Public attitudes toward migration are shaped by a complex interplay of factors. Economic concerns about competition for jobs and pressure on public services are common, though research consistently shows that the actual economic impacts of migration are generally small and often positive. Cultural concerns about social cohesion and national identity are often more powerful drivers of attitudes than economic considerations. Effective communication about the realities of migration, as opposed to the often-misleading narratives that dominate political discourse, is essential for building public support for humane and evidence-based migration policies.

translation.chineseText: 中国古建筑是中华文明的重要组成部分，体现了中国古代劳动人民的智慧和审美。从宏伟的故宫到精巧的苏州园林，从庄严的寺庙到古朴的民居，中国古建筑以其独特的风格和精湛的工艺闻名于世。中国古建筑注重与自然环境的和谐统一，讲究天人合一的哲学理念。木结构、斗拱、飞檐等建筑特色展现了极高的艺术价值。近年来，越来越多的古建筑得到修缮和保护，让这些珍贵的文化遗产得以传承。
translation.englishAnswer: Ancient Chinese architecture is an important part of Chinese civilization, embodying the wisdom and aesthetic taste of the ancient Chinese people. From the magnificent Forbidden City to the exquisite Suzhou gardens, from solemn temples to rustic folk houses, ancient Chinese architecture is renowned worldwide for its unique style and exquisite craftsmanship. Ancient Chinese architecture emphasizes harmony with the natural environment and embodies the philosophical concept of unity between heaven and humanity. Architectural features such as wooden structures, dougong brackets, and flying eaves demonstrate exceptional artistic value. In recent years, an increasing number of ancient buildings have been repaired and preserved, allowing these precious cultural heritages to be passed down through generations.
keyPoints: 词汇：embody, aesthetic, exquisite, craftsmanship, heritage
六级复杂句式：from...to...排比结构
文化概念：unity between heaven and humanity（天人合一）
建筑术语：dougong brackets（斗拱）, flying eaves（飞檐）
translation.explanation: 本题主题为中国古建筑，是六级翻译中文化类典型题目。注意：1) "天人合一"是哲学概念，译为unity between heaven and humanity；2) "斗拱"是建筑专有名词，译为dougong brackets；3) "飞檐"译为flying eaves；4) "传承"译为passed down through generations。

listeningSections.title: Conversation One
listeningSections.script:
W: Today we're speaking with Dr. Robert Lee about the global water crisis. Dr. Lee, how serious is the situation?
M: It's more serious than most people realize. Currently, over 2 billion people live in water-stressed countries. By 2025, half of the world's population could be living in water-scarce areas. Climate change is exacerbating the problem by altering precipitation patterns and causing more frequent droughts.
W: What are the main causes of water scarcity?
M: There are several interconnected factors. Agriculture accounts for about 70% of global freshwater withdrawal, and inefficient irrigation practices waste enormous amounts of water. Population growth and economic development increase demand. Pollution from industrial and agricultural sources contaminates available water supplies. And aging infrastructure in many cities leads to significant water loss through leaks.
W: Are there technological solutions to these problems?
M: Yes, technology plays an important role. Desalination technology has improved, though it remains energy-intensive. Smart water management systems using sensors and data analytics can detect leaks and optimize distribution. Wastewater treatment and recycling technologies allow us to reuse water multiple times. Drip irrigation can dramatically reduce agricultural water use.
W: What about policy approaches?
M: Proper pricing of water is essential. When water is undervalued, it tends to be wasted. Many countries are implementing integrated water resource management approaches that consider the entire water cycle. International cooperation on shared water resources is also critical, as more than 260 river basins cross national boundaries.

listeningSections.title: Conversation Two
listeningSections.script:
M: Professor Garcia, your research on the psychology of social media is fascinating. What drives people to constantly check their social media accounts?
W: It's a combination of psychological factors. The variable reward system is key — we never know when we'll receive a like, comment, or message, which creates a dopamine-driven feedback loop. Social validation plays a huge role, as does the fear of missing out, or FOMO. These mechanisms are deliberately designed by platforms to maximize engagement.
M: What are the psychological effects of heavy social media use?
W: Research has found correlations between heavy social media use and increased rates of anxiety, depression, and loneliness, particularly among adolescents. Social comparison is a major factor — people tend to present idealized versions of their lives online, leading others to feel inadequate by comparison. However, it's important to note that correlation doesn't necessarily imply causation.
M: Are there any positive effects?
W: Absolutely. Social media can provide social support, especially for individuals from marginalized groups who may not have access to supportive communities in their offline lives. It can facilitate political mobilization and raise awareness about important issues. It also helps people maintain relationships across geographical distances.
M: What advice would you give for healthier social media use?
W: I recommend being intentional about your usage. Set time limits, curate your feed to include content that genuinely adds value, and take regular breaks. The goal isn't to abandon social media entirely but to use it in a way that serves your well-being rather than undermines it.

C. Social comparison

listeningSections.title: Passage One
listeningSections.script:
The concept of lifelong learning has become increasingly important in an era of rapid technological change. As automation and artificial intelligence transform industries, the skills that workers possess today may become obsolete within a few years. The idea that education ends with formal schooling is no longer viable in the modern economy.

The economic imperative for lifelong learning is clear. According to the World Economic Forum, by 2025, 50% of all employees will need reskilling due to technological adoption. Jobs that didn't exist a decade ago, such as social media manager and data scientist, are now commonplace. The half-life of professional skills has decreased from an estimated 30 years in the mid-20th century to just five years today in many technical fields.

Lifelong learning takes many forms. Formal education includes university courses, professional certifications, and graduate degrees. Informal learning encompasses online courses, workshops, webinars, and on-the-job training. The rise of massive open online courses, or MOOCs, has democratized access to education, allowing anyone with an internet connection to learn from top universities around the world.

However, significant barriers to lifelong learning exist. Cost is a major obstacle, as is time constraints for working adults. Motivation can be difficult to sustain without external accountability. Employers play a crucial role in supporting lifelong learning through tuition reimbursement programs, dedicated learning time, and creating a culture that values continuous development. Governments can support lifelong learning through tax incentives, public education programs, and partnerships with industry to identify future skill needs.

B. 15 years
C. 10 years

listeningSections.title: Passage Two
listeningSections.script:
The study of happiness has become a serious academic discipline, with researchers from psychology, neuroscience, economics, and sociology all contributing to our understanding of what makes people happy. Perhaps the most surprising finding is that happiness is not primarily determined by external circumstances.

Research shows that genetics accounts for about 50% of our happiness set point. Circumstances — including income, marital status, and material possessions — account for only about 10%. The remaining 40% is determined by intentional activities and habits. This means that individuals have significant control over their own happiness through their choices and behaviors.

One of the most robust findings in happiness research is the importance of social connections. People with strong relationships are consistently happier than those without, regardless of their income or health status. The Harvard Study of Adult Development, which has followed participants for over 80 years, found that the quality of relationships is the single strongest predictor of happiness and health in later life.

Other factors that consistently correlate with happiness include practicing gratitude, engaging in acts of kindness, pursuing meaningful goals, and being fully engaged in activities — a state known as flow. Interestingly, research has found that beyond a certain point, additional income has diminishing returns on happiness. Once basic needs are met, relative income matters more than absolute income, and experiences tend to bring more lasting happiness than material possessions.

questions.explanation: 研究表明约50%由基因决定，10%由环境决定，40%由有意识的活动和习惯决定。

D. Physical health

readingPassages.title: Section A - 选词填空
readingPassages.content: The rise of the gig economy has fundamentally altered the landscape of employment in many countries. Characterized by short-term, flexible work arrangements mediated by digital platforms, the gig economy now (2)______ a significant and growing portion of the workforce. While this new model of work offers flexibility and autonomy, it also raises profound questions about labor rights, social protection, and economic security. Proponents of the gig economy highlight the freedom it provides. Workers can choose when, where, and how much they work, (1)______ their work to fit around other commitments such as education, caregiving, or multiple jobs. For those who value flexibility above stability, gig work can be an attractive option. Digital platforms have lowered barriers to entry, allowing people to start earning quickly without the (3)______ of traditional hiring processes. However, the gig economy also has a darker side. Gig workers are typically (4)______ as independent contractors rather than employees, which means they are not entitled to minimum wage guarantees, paid sick leave, vacation time, or employer-provided health insurance. This (5)______ of traditional employment protections leaves many gig workers vulnerable to income instability and financial insecurity. Studies have shown that after accounting for expenses, many gig workers earn below the minimum wage. The COVID-19 pandemic (6)______ many of these vulnerabilities. Gig workers faced increased health risks without access to paid sick leave, and many saw their incomes (7)______ as demand for their services fluctuated unpredictably. The pandemic also highlighted the essential nature of many gig workers, who delivered food, provided transportation, and performed other critical services during lockdowns. This (8)______ led to increased calls for policy reforms to extend labor protections to gig workers. Policy responses to the gig economy have varied across jurisdictions. Some countries have passed legislation to (9)______ gig workers as employees, entitling them to the same protections as traditional workers. Others have created a third category of worker with intermediate protections. Technology companies have (10)______ these reforms, arguing that they would undermine the flexibility that makes gig work attractive. The debate over the rights of gig workers is likely to intensify as this form of work continues to grow.

readingPassages.title: Section B - 长篇阅读匹配
readingPassages.content:
A) The COVID-19 pandemic has been the most significant global health crisis in a century, but its impacts extend far beyond health. The pandemic exposed and exacerbated existing inequalities in societies around the world, creating what many have called a twin pandemic of COVID-19 and inequality. Understanding how the pandemic affected different groups differently is essential for building more resilient and equitable societies.

B) The economic impacts of the pandemic were profoundly uneven. While many white-collar workers were able to transition to remote work with their incomes largely intact, workers in service industries, hospitality, retail, and entertainment faced massive job losses. Low-wage workers were far more likely to lose their jobs than high-wage workers. Women were disproportionately affected, as they are overrepresented in the sectors most impacted by lockdowns and also bore the brunt of increased caregiving responsibilities as schools and daycare centers closed.

C) The educational impacts of the pandemic are likely to have lasting consequences. School closures affected over 1.6 billion students worldwide, disrupting learning and social development. Students from disadvantaged backgrounds were hit hardest, lacking access to online learning resources, quiet study spaces, and parental support. Learning losses during the pandemic were most severe for students from low-income families, potentially widening educational inequalities for years to come. The digital divide in education became a visible and urgent problem.

D) Health impacts of the pandemic were also unequally distributed. In many countries, racial and ethnic minorities experienced higher rates of infection, hospitalization, and death from COVID-19. These disparities reflected underlying inequalities in living conditions, employment, access to healthcare, and prevalence of underlying health conditions. The pandemic also had significant mental health consequences, with rates of anxiety and depression increasing substantially, particularly among young people and those facing economic hardship.

E) The pandemic accelerated several pre-existing trends in profound ways. Remote work, which had been slowly growing for years, became the norm for many workers almost overnight. E-commerce experienced a dramatic surge as lockdowns prevented in-person shopping. Digital payment systems, telemedicine, and online education all saw rapid adoption. These changes have persisted beyond the acute phase of the pandemic and are likely to have lasting effects on how we work, shop, learn, and access healthcare.

F) Government responses to the pandemic varied enormously across countries, with significant consequences for health outcomes and economic impacts. Countries that acted quickly with testing, tracing, and public health measures generally fared better than those that delayed. The scale of fiscal stimulus also varied dramatically. Some countries provided substantial income support to workers and businesses, mitigating the economic impact, while others provided minimal support, leaving many people without a safety net.

G) The pandemic highlighted the critical importance of public health infrastructure and the dangers of underinvestment in health systems. Countries with strong public health systems and universal healthcare were better able to respond to the crisis. The rapid development of vaccines demonstrated the potential of scientific research when adequately funded, but the inequitable distribution of vaccines between wealthy and poor countries revealed the limits of global solidarity.

H) Social connections and community resilience proved to be important buffers against the worst effects of the pandemic. Communities with strong social networks and mutual aid systems were better able to support vulnerable members during lockdowns. The pandemic also sparked innovation in social connection, with people finding new ways to maintain relationships through digital tools, outdoor gatherings, and community initiatives.

I) The pandemic has been described as a historic event that will shape societies for generations. Its full impacts will not be understood for years, but early evidence suggests that it has accelerated many existing trends, from digitalization to inequality. The choices made during the recovery period will determine whether we emerge from this crisis with a more equitable society or one in which inequalities have become even more entrenched.

J) Building back better requires intentional policy choices. Investing in universal healthcare, strengthening social safety nets, addressing the digital divide, and creating quality jobs are essential for building resilience to future crises. The pandemic has shown that rapid, transformative change is possible when there is political will and social consensus. The challenge is to apply this lesson to the ongoing challenges of inequality, climate change, and other systemic risks.

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage One
readingPassages.content:
The debate over whether higher education is a public good or a private benefit has significant implications for how universities are funded and governed. Those who view higher education primarily as a private benefit argue that individuals who obtain degrees reap the majority of the rewards through higher earnings and better career opportunities. From this perspective, it is reasonable that students should bear a substantial portion of the costs through tuition fees. This view has been influential in shaping higher education policy in many countries, particularly the United States and the United Kingdom.

However, a growing body of evidence supports the view that higher education generates substantial public benefits that extend beyond the individual graduate. University-educated citizens tend to pay more in taxes over their lifetimes, are less likely to rely on social welfare programs, and are more likely to engage in civic activities such as voting and volunteering. Research has shown that higher levels of educational attainment in a population are associated with lower crime rates, improved public health, and greater social cohesion. These spillover effects benefit society as a whole, not just those who attend university.

The public benefits of research universities are particularly significant. Basic research conducted at universities has been the foundation for countless innovations, from the internet to mRNA vaccines. University research contributes to economic growth, technological advancement, and solutions to societal challenges. The knowledge created at universities is a public good that can be shared and built upon, generating benefits that far exceed the costs of the research.

The balance between public and private funding has shifted dramatically in many countries over the past few decades. In the United States, state funding for public universities has declined substantially, leading to significant increases in tuition. This shift has contributed to the student debt crisis and has raised concerns about access and equity. Students from low-income families are particularly affected, as the rising cost of higher education may deter them from attending or burden them with unsustainable debt.

Reversing these trends would require a renewed commitment to the idea that higher education is a public good worthy of substantial public investment. Countries that invest generously in higher education tend to have higher attainment rates and lower levels of inequality. The challenge is to build political support for such investments in an era of competing demands for public resources. The evidence suggests that the returns on investment in higher education, both private and public, are substantial enough to justify increased public funding.

readingPassages.title: Section C - 仔细阅读 Passage Two
readingPassages.content:
The concept of sustainable fashion has gained significant traction in recent years as consumers and industry leaders alike have become increasingly aware of the environmental and social costs of fast fashion. The fashion industry is responsible for approximately 10% of global carbon emissions and is the second-largest consumer of water worldwide. Additionally, the rise of fast fashion has led to a dramatic increase in textile waste, with the equivalent of one garbage truck of textiles being landfilled or burned every second.

Sustainable fashion encompasses a range of practices aimed at reducing the industry's environmental footprint and improving social conditions in the supply chain. These include using eco-friendly materials such as organic cotton, recycled polyester, and innovative plant-based alternatives. Some brands are exploring circular fashion models, where clothes are designed to be recycled or biodegraded at the end of their life, rather than ending up in landfills. Others are adopting slow fashion principles, producing fewer but higher-quality pieces designed to last longer.

Transparency and traceability have become key concerns for sustainable fashion advocates. Consumers are increasingly demanding to know where their clothes come from, who made them, and under what conditions. In response, some brands are using blockchain technology to create transparent supply chains that allow consumers to trace a garment's journey from raw material to finished product. Certifications such as Fair Trade, GOTS (Global Organic Textile Standard), and B Corp help consumers identify brands that meet certain environmental and social standards.

The sustainable fashion movement faces significant challenges, however. Sustainable materials and ethical production methods are often more expensive, making sustainable fashion less accessible to price-sensitive consumers. There is also the challenge of greenwashing, where brands make misleading claims about their environmental practices to appeal to eco-conscious consumers without making meaningful changes. Critics argue that the most sustainable approach is simply to buy less clothing, regardless of what materials are used.

Despite these challenges, the momentum behind sustainable fashion continues to grow. Major fashion brands have made public commitments to sustainability, and innovative startups are developing new materials and business models. The rise of resale and rental platforms has created alternative ways to consume fashion that are less resource-intensive. While there is no single solution to the fashion industry's environmental problems, the growing awareness and action across the industry represent meaningful progress toward a more sustainable future.

questions.questionText: What environmental problems does the fashion industry cause according to the passage?

D. It has no significant environmental impact.

translation.chineseText: 中国教育事业的发展取得了举世瞩目的成就。改革开放以来，中国建立了世界上最大的教育体系，实现了九年义务教育的全面普及。高等教育毛入学率已超过50%，进入普及化阶段。中国政府高度重视教育公平，通过实施农村义务教育经费保障机制、资助家庭经济困难学生等政策措施，努力缩小城乡和区域之间的教育差距。同时，职业教育的发展也为国家经济建设培养了大批高素质技术技能人才。教育现代化是中华民族伟大复兴的重要支撑。
translation.englishAnswer: The development of China's education sector has achieved remarkable accomplishments that have attracted worldwide attention. Since the reform and opening-up, China has established the world's largest education system and achieved universal coverage of nine-year compulsory education. The gross enrollment rate of higher education has exceeded 50%, entering the stage of popularization. The Chinese government attaches great importance to educational equity, striving to narrow the educational gap between urban and rural areas and among different regions through policy measures such as implementing the funding guarantee mechanism for rural compulsory education and providing financial assistance to students from economically disadvantaged families. Meanwhile, vocational education has cultivated a large number of high-quality technical and skilled talents for national economic development. The modernization of education is an important pillar for the great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation.
keyPoints: 词汇：remarkable, universal coverage, gross enrollment rate, compulsory education, equity
六级复杂句式：分词结构（striving to...）、such as举例结构
专有名词：nine-year compulsory education（九年义务教育）
政策术语：funding guarantee mechanism（经费保障机制）
translation.explanation: 本题主题为中国教育发展，是六级翻译中社会热点类典型题目。注意：1) "举世瞩目"译为attracted worldwide attention；2) "毛入学率"译为gross enrollment rate；3) "九年义务教育"译为nine-year compulsory education；4) "教育公平"译为educational equity；5) "中华民族伟大复兴"译为great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation。

listeningSections.title: Conversation One
listeningSections.script:
W: Professor Thompson, your research on space exploration and its benefits to humanity is very inspiring. Why should we invest in space programs when there are so many problems on Earth?
M: That's a common question. The answer is that space exploration is not a luxury but an investment that yields tremendous returns. The technologies developed for space programs have led to countless spin-off innovations that benefit life on Earth, from memory foam and GPS to water purification systems and medical imaging technologies.
W: What are the most promising areas of space exploration right now?
M: Mars exploration is obviously a major focus, with both NASA and private companies working toward crewed missions. But I'm particularly excited about the search for exoplanets. The James Webb Space Telescope is providing unprecedented views of distant worlds and could help answer fundamental questions about the possibility of life elsewhere in the universe.
W: How does space exploration address climate change?
M: Satellites are essential for climate monitoring. They track deforestation, measure ice melt, monitor atmospheric composition, and provide data for weather prediction. Without space-based observations, our understanding of climate change would be severely limited. Additionally, research on closed-loop life support systems for space missions has applications for sustainable living on Earth.
W: What about the commercial space sector?
M: The commercial space industry has grown enormously. Companies like SpaceX have dramatically reduced launch costs, making space more accessible. This has enabled new applications in satellite communications, Earth observation, and even space tourism. The space economy is projected to grow to over one trillion dollars in the coming decades.

listeningSections.title: Conversation Two
listeningSections.script:
M: Dr. Kim, you're an expert in food science and nutrition. What are the biggest challenges facing our global food system?
W: The challenges are immense. We need to feed a growing global population that is expected to reach nearly 10 billion by 2050, while simultaneously reducing the environmental impact of food production. The current food system is responsible for about one-third of global greenhouse gas emissions and uses 70% of freshwater resources.
M: What innovations are most promising for sustainable food production?
W: Plant-based proteins and cultivated meat are gaining traction as alternatives to traditional animal agriculture, which has a particularly high environmental footprint. Vertical farming allows year-round production of fresh produce in urban areas with minimal water and land use. Precision agriculture uses sensors, drones, and AI to optimize crop yields while reducing inputs like fertilizer and pesticides.
M: What about food waste? That's a huge issue.
W: Approximately one-third of all food produced globally is wasted. This is not only an economic loss but also a waste of all the resources used in production. Solutions include better supply chain management, improved storage technologies, and consumer education. Some countries have implemented policies requiring supermarkets to donate unsold food to charities.
M: How can individuals contribute to a more sustainable food system?
W: Individuals can make a significant impact by reducing food waste, choosing more plant-based meals, buying locally when possible, and being mindful of packaging. While individual actions matter, systemic changes in policy and industry practices are also essential for creating a truly sustainable food system.

listeningSections.title: Passage One
listeningSections.script:
The phenomenon of urbanization is one of the most significant demographic trends of our time. For the first time in human history, more than half of the world's population lives in urban areas, and this proportion continues to grow. By 2050, nearly 70% of the global population is expected to be urban, with most of this growth occurring in Asia and Africa.

Cities offer numerous advantages that attract people. They concentrate economic opportunities, cultural amenities, and social services. Productivity is higher in cities due to agglomeration effects — when people and businesses are close together, ideas spread more quickly, labor markets are more efficient, and specialized services become viable. Cities also tend to have lower per capita carbon footprints than suburban or rural areas due to shared infrastructure and more efficient transportation.

However, rapid urbanization also presents significant challenges. In many developing countries, urban growth has outpaced the ability of governments to provide adequate housing, infrastructure, and services. This has led to the proliferation of informal settlements or slums, where residents lack access to clean water, sanitation, and secure tenure. Traffic congestion, air pollution, and waste management are persistent problems in many rapidly growing cities.

The concept of smart cities has emerged as a potential solution to urban challenges. Smart cities use digital technology and data analytics to improve the efficiency of urban services, from transportation and energy to waste management and public safety. However, critics caution that smart city initiatives must prioritize equity and privacy, ensuring that technological solutions benefit all residents, not just the wealthy. The most successful cities will be those that combine technological innovation with inclusive planning and good governance.

listeningSections.title: Passage Two
listeningSections.script:
The study of climate change psychology examines why people fail to act on climate change despite widespread awareness of the problem. Understanding these psychological barriers is essential for designing effective communication strategies and policies to promote climate action.

One major barrier is psychological distance. Many people perceive climate change as a problem that is distant in time, affecting future generations rather than themselves, and distant in space, affecting polar bears and distant islands rather than their own communities. This perception reduces the sense of urgency. Climate change communication increasingly focuses on local, immediate impacts to overcome this barrier.

Another barrier is the nature of the threat itself. Climate change is a slow-moving, complex problem with uncertain outcomes. Unlike immediate threats, it doesn't trigger our fight-or-flight response. The causes and effects are often invisible, and the benefits of action are diffuse and long-term, while the costs are immediate and concentrated. This makes it politically challenging to implement climate policies.

Cognitive biases also play a role. Optimism bias leads people to believe that they will be less affected than others. Confirmation bias causes people to seek out information that confirms their existing beliefs about climate change. Motivated reasoning leads people to reject evidence that conflicts with their values or identity.

Despite these barriers, there is reason for optimism. Social norms are shifting rapidly as climate action becomes more mainstream. Effective communication that emphasizes shared values, provides clear pathways for action, and highlights the co-benefits of climate solutions has been shown to increase engagement. The psychology of climate change reminds us that addressing this challenge requires not only technological solutions but also a deep understanding of human behavior.

readingPassages.content: The concept of work-life balance has become a central concern for employees and employers alike in the modern workplace. As technology has blurred the boundaries between work and personal life, achieving a healthy balance has become increasingly (1)______. The pandemic accelerated this trend, with remote work making it even harder for many people to disconnect from their jobs. Research has shown that poor work-life balance leads to burnout, decreased productivity, and negative health outcomes. Organizations that (2)______ employee well-being through flexible policies and reasonable workloads tend to have lower turnover rates and higher job satisfaction. However, creating a culture that truly supports balance requires more than just policies on paper. Managers must be trained to (3)______ signs of stress and burnout in their team members. Employees need to feel (4)______ to set boundaries without fear of negative consequences. The physical workspace, whether at home or in the office, also plays a role in supporting well-being. Some companies have (5)______ four-day work weeks with promising results, reporting maintained or increased productivity alongside improved employee satisfaction. Critics argue that work-life balance is a privilege primarily (6)______ to knowledge workers, while many service and manufacturing jobs offer little flexibility. This raises questions about equity and the need for broader labor market reforms. Technology can be both a problem and a solution. While constant connectivity creates pressure to be always available, digital tools can also (7)______ workflows and reduce unnecessary meetings. The key is intentional use of technology rather than letting it (8)______ our attention. As the nature of work continues to evolve, the pursuit of work-life balance will remain a (9)______ challenge. There is no one-size-fits-all solution, as different individuals and industries have different needs. What matters most is ongoing dialogue between employers and employees to create work (10)______ that support both organizational success and human well-being.
readingPassages.title: Section A - 选词填空

readingPassages.content:
A) The rise of e-commerce has fundamentally transformed the retail landscape over the past two decades. Online shopping, once a novelty, has become a mainstream mode of consumption, accounting for an increasing share of retail sales worldwide. This transformation has been driven by advances in technology, changes in consumer behavior, and significant investments by both established retailers and new entrants.

B) The convenience of e-commerce is perhaps its most significant advantage. Consumers can shop from anywhere at any time, compare prices across multiple sellers instantly, and have products delivered to their doorsteps. The rise of mobile commerce has made this convenience even more accessible, with smartphones enabling purchases with just a few taps. Features such as one-click ordering and saved payment information have reduced friction in the purchasing process.

C) However, the growth of e-commerce has had profound impacts on physical retail. The phenomenon known as the retail apocalypse has seen thousands of brick-and-mortar stores close, with iconic brands declaring bankruptcy and shopping malls struggling to maintain occupancy rates. This has led to job losses and the decline of traditional shopping districts in many communities.

D) The environmental impact of e-commerce is a growing concern. While online shopping can reduce the carbon footprint of individual shopping trips, the convenience of fast delivery has led to increased packaging waste and emissions from delivery vehicles. The rise of quick delivery promises, including same-day and even one-hour delivery, has particularly exacerbated these environmental costs.

E) Supply chain and logistics have become critical competitive factors in e-commerce. Companies like Amazon have set new standards for delivery speed and reliability, forcing competitors to invest heavily in warehousing, delivery networks, and technology. The concept of last-mile delivery has become a key focus, as getting products from distribution centers to customers' homes is often the most expensive and complex part of the supply chain.

F) Consumer data has become a valuable asset in the e-commerce ecosystem. Online retailers collect vast amounts of data on customer preferences, browsing behavior, and purchase history, using this information to personalize recommendations and target marketing. However, concerns about data privacy and security have led to increased regulation, such as the GDPR in Europe and similar laws in other jurisdictions.

G) Small businesses have both benefited and struggled in the e-commerce era. Platforms like Shopify and Etsy have made it easier for small sellers to reach customers worldwide, lowering barriers to entry. However, competing with large platforms that offer lower prices and faster shipping remains challenging. The dominance of major e-commerce platforms has raised antitrust concerns in several countries.

H) The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated e-commerce adoption dramatically. Consumers who had previously been reluctant to shop online were forced to try it during lockdowns, and many continued afterward. This permanent shift in consumer behavior has led retailers to rethink their strategies, with many investing in omnichannel approaches that integrate online and offline experiences.

I) Social commerce represents the next frontier of e-commerce. Platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and Pinterest have integrated shopping features, allowing users to discover and purchase products without leaving the app. Live streaming shopping, popularized in China, is gaining traction in Western markets, combining entertainment with instant purchasing.

J) Looking ahead, several trends will shape the future of e-commerce. Artificial intelligence will enable more sophisticated personalization and customer service through chatbots. Augmented reality will allow consumers to try products virtually before purchasing. Drone delivery and autonomous vehicles may transform last-mile logistics. The boundaries between online and offline retail will continue to blur as technology creates seamless shopping experiences.

readingPassages.content:
The debate over genetic engineering has intensified as technologies like CRISPR have made gene editing more accessible and precise than ever before. Proponents argue that these technologies hold the potential to eliminate genetic diseases, increase crop yields, and address some of humanity's most pressing challenges. Critics, however, raise ethical concerns about the consequences of altering the fundamental building blocks of life.

In medicine, gene therapy offers hope for treating previously incurable genetic disorders. Clinical trials have shown remarkable success in treating conditions such as sickle cell disease and certain forms of blindness. The potential to edit genes in embryos, however, raises particularly controversial questions. In 2018, a Chinese scientist announced the birth of the first gene-edited babies, sparking international outrage and calls for a moratorium on heritable gene editing. The scientific community has largely condemned this experiment as premature and unethical, highlighting the need for clear international guidelines.

In agriculture, genetically modified crops have been widely adopted, with millions of farmers growing GM crops on over 190 million hectares worldwide. These crops offer benefits such as pest resistance, drought tolerance, and enhanced nutritional content. Golden Rice, engineered to produce beta-carotene, has the potential to address vitamin A deficiency, which causes blindness and death in hundreds of thousands of children annually in developing countries. Despite these benefits, GM crops face significant consumer resistance in many regions, particularly in Europe, where concerns about environmental impacts and corporate control of the food supply have led to strict regulations.

The regulatory landscape for genetic technologies varies widely across countries. The United States tends to take a more permissive approach, regulating based on the characteristics of the final product rather than the process used to create it. Europe, in contrast, has taken a more precautionary approach, with the European Court of Justice ruling that organisms edited with CRISPR should be subject to the same regulations as traditional GMOs. These regulatory differences have significant implications for research, innovation, and trade.

Looking forward, the challenge lies in developing governance frameworks that allow beneficial applications of genetic technologies to proceed while preventing misuse. Many experts advocate for inclusive public dialogue that engages citizens in discussions about the risks and benefits, rather than leaving these decisions solely to scientists and policymakers. The future of genetic engineering will depend not only on scientific advances but also on society's ability to navigate the complex ethical terrain that these technologies present.

readingPassages.content:
The phenomenon of fake news and misinformation has become a central concern in contemporary discussions about democracy and public discourse. While false information has always existed, the digital age has amplified its reach and impact to an unprecedented degree. Understanding the mechanisms behind the spread of misinformation is essential for developing effective countermeasures.

Social media platforms have been identified as primary vectors for the spread of misinformation. The algorithms that power these platforms are designed to maximize engagement, and sensational, emotionally charged content consistently generates more engagement than factual, balanced reporting. A study by MIT found that false news spreads significantly farther, faster, and more broadly than true news on Twitter, with falsehoods being 70% more likely to be retweeted than accurate information. The researchers attributed this to the novelty of false information, which tends to be more surprising and therefore more shareable.

The psychological factors that make people susceptible to misinformation have been extensively studied. Confirmation bias leads individuals to accept information that confirms their existing beliefs while critically scrutinizing information that contradicts them. The illusory truth effect demonstrates that repeated exposure to a claim increases the likelihood of believing it, regardless of its accuracy. Emotional states also play a role, with anxiety and anger increasing susceptibility to misinformation. Furthermore, motivated reasoning causes people to process information in ways that serve their preexisting goals and identities.

Efforts to combat misinformation have taken multiple approaches. Fact-checking organizations have proliferated, but their effectiveness is limited by the fact that corrections often reach far fewer people than the original false claims. Media literacy education aims to equip individuals with critical thinking skills to evaluate information sources. Some platforms have implemented algorithmic changes to reduce the visibility of false content and have partnered with fact-checkers to label disputed information. However, these efforts have been criticized by those who see them as forms of censorship.

The challenge of addressing misinformation is compounded by the tension between combating false information and protecting free expression. Democratic societies must navigate this tension carefully, as heavy-handed approaches risk suppressing legitimate speech. The most promising strategies combine technological solutions, educational initiatives, and efforts to rebuild trust in reliable information sources. Ultimately, addressing misinformation requires not only technical fixes but also addressing the underlying social and political divisions that make people receptive to false narratives.

listeningSections.title: Conversation One
listeningSections.script:
W: Professor Adams, thank you for speaking with us about workplace mental health. What are the key factors affecting mental health at work?
M: Several factors are crucial. Job control is one — when employees have little say over how they do their work, stress levels increase. Workload is another obvious factor, but interestingly, underload can be as problematic as overload. Social support from colleagues and managers is a powerful buffer against stress. And perhaps most importantly, perceived fairness and respect in the workplace strongly predict mental health outcomes.
W: How has the pandemic changed workplace mental health?
M: The pandemic has had mixed effects. For some, remote work reduced commuting stress and provided more flexibility. For others, it blurred boundaries and increased isolation. We've seen a significant increase in reported anxiety and depression. However, the pandemic also reduced stigma around mental health discussions, as more people openly acknowledged struggling.
W: What can employers do to support mental health?
M: The most effective approaches are comprehensive. Employee assistance programs provide counseling services. Flexible work arrangements can help employees manage their responsibilities. Training managers to recognize signs of distress is crucial. But the foundation must be a culture that values well-being over presenteeism — being present at work regardless of health status.
W: What about individual strategies?
M: Individuals can practice setting boundaries between work and personal time. Regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and maintaining social connections are all protective factors. Mindfulness and stress management techniques can help. But we must be careful not to place all responsibility on individuals — employers have a duty to create healthy work environments.

listeningSections.script:
M: Dr. Foster, many countries have set ambitious targets for renewable energy. How realistic are these goals?
W: They are ambitious but achievable with sufficient political will and investment. The technology exists. Solar and wind are now the cheapest sources of electricity in many markets. The main challenges are grid integration, storage, and the political economy of transitioning away from fossil fuels.
M: What about nuclear power? Does it have a role?
W: Nuclear provides reliable baseload power with zero carbon emissions. Some see it as essential for meeting climate targets. However, the high cost, long construction times, and unresolved waste disposal issues remain significant barriers. Small modular reactors might address some of these concerns, but they're still in development.
M: How important is energy storage for renewable integration?
W: Storage is absolutely critical. Battery technology has improved dramatically, with costs falling by nearly 90% over the past decade. But we need much more storage capacity. Pumped hydro, compressed air, and green hydrogen are other storage options being developed.
M: What policy measures are most effective?
W: Carbon pricing creates a level playing field for clean energy. Renewable portfolio standards mandate a certain percentage of electricity from renewable sources. Feed-in tariffs provide price certainty for renewable producers. But perhaps most important is ending fossil fuel subsidies, which amounted to over 5 trillion dollars globally in 2022.

listeningSections.title: Passage One
listeningSections.script:
The science of habit formation has important implications for anyone trying to make lasting changes in their lives. Habits are automatic behaviors triggered by contextual cues, developed through repetition and reinforcement. Understanding how habits work can help individuals build good habits and break bad ones more effectively.

Charles Duhigg, in his book The Power of Habit, popularized the habit loop model, which consists of three components: the cue, the routine, and the reward. The cue is a trigger that tells your brain to go into automatic mode. The routine is the behavior itself. The reward is the positive feeling or benefit you get from the behavior. Over time, your brain begins to associate the cue with the reward, creating a craving that drives the habit loop.

James Clear, author of Atomic Habits, has further developed our understanding of habit formation. His four laws of behavior change provide a practical framework: make it obvious, make it attractive, make it easy, and make it satisfying. Clear emphasizes the power of small changes, arguing that improving by just 1% each day leads to significant results over time through the compound effect. He also stresses the importance of identity-based habits, where you focus on becoming the type of person who embodies the habit rather than just achieving a specific outcome.

Research has shown that habit formation typically takes longer than the popular 21-day figure suggests. A study by University College London found that it takes an average of 66 days for a new behavior to become automatic, though the range varies from 18 to 254 days depending on the complexity of the behavior and individual differences. The key to successful habit formation is consistency, particularly in the early stages when the behavior requires conscious effort.

listeningSections.title: Passage Two
listeningSections.script:
Global food security is one of the most pressing challenges of the twenty-first century. Despite producing enough food to feed the global population, nearly 800 million people experience chronic hunger, and over 2 billion suffer from micronutrient deficiencies. The problem is not simply about the quantity of food produced but about access, distribution, and sustainability.

Climate change poses a direct threat to food security. Rising temperatures, changing precipitation patterns, and more frequent extreme weather events are already affecting crop yields in many regions. Studies suggest that without adaptation, global crop yields could decline by 5-30% by 2050. The impacts are expected to be most severe in tropical and subtropical regions, where many developing countries with high food insecurity are located. This creates a cruel paradox: those least responsible for climate change are often most vulnerable to its effects on food production.

Food waste is another critical issue. Approximately one-third of all food produced globally is lost or wasted, representing not only a waste of food but also a waste of all the resources used in production, including water, land, and energy. In developed countries, most waste occurs at the consumer level, while in developing countries, it occurs primarily during production, storage, and transportation due to inadequate infrastructure.

Solutions to food insecurity require a multi-faceted approach. Sustainable intensification aims to increase crop yields on existing farmland while minimizing environmental impacts. Improving storage and transportation infrastructure can reduce post-harvest losses. Social protection programs, such as school feeding programs and cash transfers, help vulnerable populations access food. International trade also plays a crucial role in moving food from surplus regions to deficit regions. Ultimately, achieving food security for all will require coordinated action across multiple sectors and at all levels from local to global.

readingPassages.content: The phenomenon of global migration has become one of the defining issues of the twenty-first century. People move across borders for a variety of reasons, including economic opportunity, political instability, and environmental change. International migration has both positive and negative effects on sending and receiving countries. On the positive side, migration can (1)______ labor shortages in receiving countries and bring economic benefits through diversity and innovation. Migrants often fill essential roles in healthcare, agriculture, and technology sectors. Remittances sent back to home countries (2)______ billions of dollars to developing economies, often exceeding foreign aid. However, migration also presents challenges. Receiving countries may face pressure on public services and infrastructure. There can be social tensions related to cultural differences and integration. Sending countries may experience brain drain as skilled workers (3)______ opportunities abroad. The treatment of migrants is a significant human rights concern, with many migrants facing exploitation, discrimination, and difficult living conditions. Climate change is (4)______ to become a major driver of migration in the coming decades, as rising sea levels, desertification, and extreme weather events displace populations. Some (5)______ suggest that by 2050, there could be over 200 million climate migrants. Policy responses to migration vary widely across countries. Some nations have (6)______ border controls and reduced immigration quotas, while others have created pathways for skilled migration and refugee resettlement. International cooperation is essential for managing migration effectively, as no country can address the issue alone. The Global Compact for Migration, adopted by the United Nations in 2018, provides a (7)______ for international cooperation on migration governance. Integration policies are crucial for ensuring that migrants can (8)______ fully in their new societies. Language training, employment support, and anti-discrimination measures help facilitate integration. Research shows that successful integration benefits both migrants and receiving communities. The economic (9)______ of immigration are generally positive when labor markets are flexible and integration policies are effective. As the world becomes increasingly interconnected, migration will continue to shape societies. The challenge is to manage it in ways that (10)______ the benefits while minimizing the costs, ensuring that migration is a choice, not a necessity.
readingPassages.title: Section A - 选词填空

readingPassages.content:
A) The Internet of Things represents the next phase of the digital revolution, where everyday objects are connected to the internet, collecting and sharing data. From smart thermostats and wearable fitness trackers to industrial sensors and connected vehicles, IoT devices are transforming how we live and work. The number of connected IoT devices is expected to reach over 25 billion by 2030.

B) Smart home technology is perhaps the most visible application of IoT for consumers. Smart thermostats learn household patterns and adjust temperatures automatically, reducing energy consumption. Smart security systems allow homeowners to monitor their properties remotely. Smart appliances can notify users when supplies are running low or when maintenance is needed. While these technologies offer convenience, concerns about data privacy and security remain significant.

C) In healthcare, IoT devices are enabling remote patient monitoring and telemedicine. Wearable devices can track vital signs, detect falls, and monitor chronic conditions. Smart pills with embedded sensors can confirm medication adherence. These technologies have the potential to improve patient outcomes while reducing healthcare costs, particularly for elderly patients and those with chronic conditions.

D) Industrial IoT is transforming manufacturing through what is often called Industry 4.0. Sensors on factory equipment can predict maintenance needs before breakdowns occur, reducing downtime. Connected supply chains provide real-time visibility into inventory levels and shipment status. Smart warehouses use robots and automated systems to optimize storage and retrieval. These applications increase efficiency and reduce costs.

E) Smart city initiatives use IoT technology to improve urban services. Connected traffic lights can adjust timing based on real-time traffic conditions, reducing congestion. Smart waste management systems notify collection services when bins are full. Environmental sensors monitor air quality and noise levels. These applications can improve quality of life for urban residents while reducing resource consumption.

F) Agriculture is benefiting from IoT through precision farming techniques. Soil sensors monitor moisture and nutrient levels, enabling precise irrigation and fertilization. Weather stations provide localized data for planting and harvesting decisions. Livestock tracking devices monitor animal health and location. These technologies help farmers increase yields while reducing water and chemical use.

G) Security and privacy concerns are among the biggest challenges facing IoT adoption. Each connected device represents a potential entry point for cyberattacks. Insecure IoT devices have been used in large-scale distributed denial of service attacks. Consumer IoT devices often collect personal data that could be misused. Addressing these security challenges requires industry standards, better design practices, and consumer awareness.

H) Interoperability is another significant challenge. With many different manufacturers and protocols, ensuring that IoT devices from different vendors can work together seamlessly is difficult. Industry alliances are working on common standards, but fragmentation remains a barrier to widespread adoption. The lack of interoperability can frustrate consumers and limit the potential benefits of IoT.

I) The environmental impact of IoT is a growing concern. While IoT can improve energy efficiency, the production and disposal of billions of connected devices consume resources and generate electronic waste. The energy required to power devices and transmit data also has environmental costs. Sustainable design and responsible disposal practices are increasingly important considerations.

J) Looking to the future, several trends will shape IoT development. Edge computing, where data is processed locally rather than in the cloud, will reduce latency and bandwidth requirements. Artificial intelligence will enable more sophisticated analysis of IoT data. 5G networks will provide the speed and capacity needed for大规模 IoT deployments. As these technologies converge, IoT will become more powerful, more reliable, and more integrated into our daily lives.

readingPassages.content:
The concept of corporate social responsibility has evolved from a peripheral concern to a central element of business strategy. Companies are increasingly expected to consider their impact on society and the environment, not just their financial performance. This shift reflects changing societal expectations, investor demands, and a growing recognition that sustainable business practices can be compatible with profitability.

The business case for CSR rests on several arguments. First, CSR can enhance brand reputation and consumer loyalty. Studies have shown that consumers, particularly younger generations, prefer to buy from companies that demonstrate social and environmental responsibility. Second, CSR can help attract and retain talent. Employees increasingly want to work for organizations whose values align with their own. Companies with strong CSR records tend to have higher employee engagement and lower turnover. Third, CSR can improve operational efficiency. Environmental initiatives such as reducing energy consumption and waste can lower costs. Fourth, CSR can help manage risk. Companies that proactively address environmental and social issues are better prepared for regulatory changes and less vulnerable to reputational damage from controversies.

However, CSR also faces criticism. Some argue that the primary responsibility of business is to maximize shareholder value, and that CSR distracts from this core purpose. Others point to greenwashing, where companies engage in superficial CSR activities for public relations purposes without making meaningful changes. The effectiveness of CSR is difficult to measure, and there is debate about whether voluntary corporate initiatives are sufficient or whether stronger government regulation is needed.

The concept of creating shared value, developed by Michael Porter and Mark Kramer, offers an alternative framework. CSV argues that companies can generate economic value in a way that also produces value for society by addressing societal needs and challenges. Unlike CSR, which is often seen as a separate activity, CSV is integrated into the core business strategy. Examples include developing products that address social needs, improving supply chain sustainability, and investing in cluster development in the communities where companies operate.

The future of CSR is likely to involve greater integration with core business strategy, more rigorous measurement of social and environmental impact, and increased transparency through standardized reporting frameworks. The growing emphasis on environmental, social, and governance criteria in investment decisions is putting additional pressure on companies to demonstrate genuine commitment to responsible business practices. Ultimately, the companies that thrive will be those that successfully align their business objectives with the broader interests of society.

readingPassages.content:
The phenomenon of behavioral economics has challenged traditional economic assumptions about human rationality. Traditional economic models assume that people make decisions by rationally weighing costs and benefits to maximize their utility. Behavioral economics, drawing on insights from psychology, demonstrates that human decision-making is systematically influenced by cognitive biases, emotions, and social factors.

One of the most influential concepts in behavioral economics is the nudge theory, popularized by Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein. A nudge is a subtle change in the choice architecture that influences people's behavior in predictable ways without restricting their freedom of choice. For example, automatically enrolling employees in retirement savings plans, while allowing them to opt out, significantly increases participation rates compared to requiring them to opt in. This leverages the human tendency toward inertia and defaults.

Framing effects demonstrate how the way choices are presented influences decisions. People respond differently to the same information depending on whether it is framed in terms of potential gains or potential losses. Loss aversion, a key finding in prospect theory, shows that the pain of losing is psychologically about twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining. This asymmetry explains why people are often reluctant to sell losing investments or why they stick with the status quo even when change would be beneficial.

Behavioral economics has been applied in public policy through initiatives that use nudges to improve outcomes in areas such as health, finance, and education. The UK's Behavioural Insights Team, popularly known as the Nudge Unit, has used behavioral insights to increase tax compliance, encourage organ donation, and promote energy conservation. These interventions are often low-cost and have been shown to produce significant improvements in behavior.

However, behavioral economics also raises ethical questions. Critics argue that nudges can be manipulative, particularly when they exploit cognitive biases without people's awareness. There are concerns about paternalism and the extent to which governments should influence individual choices. Proponents counter that choice architecture is inevitable — the way choices are presented always influences decisions, so the question is not whether to nudge but how to nudge ethically and transparently.
